* When backdropDismiss is false, the only way to close the modal is programmatically or via Escape key (if onRequestClose is provided). Design your content with an explicit close control.
* onShow fires after the enter animation completes, not when visible becomes true. For 'none' animation type, it fires immediately.
* Modal uses Portal internally. You do not need to wrap it in a Portal yourself.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Pressable
- Portal
- Box
- Animation


========================================
NavPanel
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Sidebar navigation panel with grouped sections, active item highlighting, and optional header slot.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

NavPanel renders a vertical sidebar navigation component organized into titled sections. Each section contains a list of selectable items. The currently active item is highlighted with a distinct background and text color, and hovering over items provides visual feedback via Pressable.

An optional header slot renders arbitrary content at the top of the panel, separated from the sections by a divider line. NavPanel uses a fixed width by default but can be customized via the `width` prop or `style` overrides.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Import: import { NavPanel } from '@reactjit/core';

NavItem type:
```ts
interface NavItem {
  id: string;
  label: string;
}
```

NavSection type:
```ts
interface NavSection {
  title: string;
  items: NavItem[];
}
```

Usage:
```tsx
<NavPanel
  sections={[
    {
      title: 'General',
      items: [
        { id: 'dashboard', label: 'Dashboard' },
        { id: 'settings', label: 'Settings' },
      ],
    },
    {
      title: 'Data',
      items: [
        { id: 'reports', label: 'Reports' },
        { id: 'analytics', label: 'Analytics' },
      ],
    },
  ]}
  activeId="dashboard"
  onSelect={(id) => setPage(id)}
/>
```

Props:

| Prop     | Type                       | Default | Description                            |
|----------|----------------------------|---------|----------------------------------------|
| sections | NavSection[]               | (required)| Array of navigation sections         |
| activeId | string                     | -       | ID of the currently active item        |
| onSelect | (id: string) => void       | -       | Called when an item is clicked         |
| header   | ReactNode                  | -       | Content rendered at the top of panel   |
| width    | number                     | 180     | Panel width in pixels                  |
| style    | Style                      | -       | Container style overrides              |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Basic sidebar navigation
--------
```tsx
const [page, setPage] = useState('home');

<NavPanel
  sections={[
    {
      title: 'Navigation',
      items: [
        { id: 'home', label: 'Home' },
        { id: 'explore', label: 'Explore' },
        { id: 'library', label: 'Library' },
      ],
    },
  ]}
  activeId={page}
  onSelect={setPage}
/>
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: With header and multiple sections
--------
```tsx
<NavPanel
  header={
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#e2e8f0', fontWeight: 'bold' }}>
      My App
    </Text>
  }
  sections={[
    {
      title: 'Main',
      items: [
        { id: 'dashboard', label: 'Dashboard' },
        { id: 'projects', label: 'Projects' },
      ],
    },
    {
      title: 'Account',
      items: [
        { id: 'profile', label: 'Profile' },
        { id: 'settings', label: 'Settings' },
      ],
    },
  ]}
  activeId="dashboard"
  onSelect={(id) => navigate(id)}
  width={200}
/>
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Full-height sidebar in layout
--------
```tsx
<Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
  <NavPanel
    sections={sections}
    activeId={currentPage}
    onSelect={setCurrentPage}
    style={{ height: '100%' }}
  />
  <Box style={{ flexGrow: 1 }}>
    {/* Page content */}
  </Box>
</Box>
```
--------
Platforms: all

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The `sections` prop is required. An empty array renders only the background panel with optional header.
* `activeId` is compared against each item's `id` for highlighting. If no item matches, no item is highlighted.
* NavPanel has a fixed `width` (default 180). It does not grow to fill its container. Override with `style={{ flexGrow: 1 }}` or set an explicit `width` if needed.
* The header slot renders any ReactNode. It is not affected by section styling.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Tabs
- Breadcrumbs
- Pressable


========================================
PieChart
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Pixel-grid pie and donut chart rendered entirely from Box primitives

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

PieChart rasterizes a pie chart into a 2x2-pixel cell grid using polar coordinate math, then renders each run of same-colored cells as a single Box. This approach requires no canvas or SVG — it works on any target that supports Box with backgroundColor. Setting innerRadius greater than zero hollows the center to produce a donut chart. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering the chart reveals a legend tooltip listing every segment's label, raw value, and percentage.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { PieChart } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Data shape:
```tsx
interface PieChartSegment {
  label: string;  // Segment name shown in the hover tooltip legend
  value: number;  // Numeric value; proportions are computed from the total
  color: Color;   // Fill color for this segment; required per segment
}
```

Props:
| Prop          | Type                   | Default    | Description                                                                     |
|---------------|------------------------|------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| data          | PieChartSegment[]      | required   | Array of segments; values are proportional, not percentages                     |
| size          | number                 | 120        | Width and height of the chart square in pixels                                  |
| innerRadius   | number                 | 0          | Hollow center radius in pixels; 0 = solid pie, >0 = donut ring                 |
| interactive   | boolean                | false      | Enable hover to show the full segment legend tooltip                            |
| style         | Style                  | undefined  | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box                            |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Basic pie chart
--------
```tsx
import { PieChart } from '@reactjit/core';

const marketShare = [
  { label: 'Chrome',  value: 65, color: '#3b82f6' },
  { label: 'Safari',  value: 19, color: '#22c55e' },
  { label: 'Firefox', value: 9,  color: '#f59e0b' },
  { label: 'Other',   value: 7,  color: '#64748b' },
];

export default function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <PieChart data={marketShare} size={160} />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Donut chart with innerRadius
--------
```tsx
import { PieChart } from '@reactjit/core';

const storage = [
  { label: 'Used',  value: 73, color: '#6366f1' },
  { label: 'Free',  value: 27, color: '#1e293b' },
];

export default function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <PieChart
        data={storage}
        size={140}
        innerRadius={40}
      />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Interactive pie with legend tooltip
--------
```tsx
import { PieChart } from '@reactjit/core';

const budget = [
  { label: 'Engineering', value: 45, color: '#3b82f6' },
  { label: 'Marketing',   value: 25, color: '#ec4899' },
  { label: 'Operations',  value: 20, color: '#f59e0b' },
  { label: 'HR',          value: 10, color: '#22c55e' },
];

export default function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <PieChart
        data={budget}
        size={160}
        innerRadius={50}
        interactive
      />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The chart is rendered as a pixel grid at 2px cell resolution. `size` must be even for the grid to divide cleanly; odd values are floored internally via `Math.floor(size / cellSize)`.
* All segment `color` values are required — there is no fallback color. Omitting `color` from a segment will render that segment transparently.
* `innerRadius` is specified in the same pixel units as `size`, not as a fraction. A `size={120}` chart with `innerRadius={60}` produces a ring exactly half the radius.
* The tooltip appears to the right of the chart (`left: '100%'`). Ensure the chart container has enough horizontal space for the tooltip or it will be clipped.
* Rasterization cost scales with `size`. Very large values (>300) will produce a high Box count; prefer sizes in the 80–200px range.
* Segments with a value of 0 are included in the legend but produce no visible arc — this is safe.
* Do not use Unicode symbols in segment `label` strings.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- RadarChart — another pixel-rasterized chart for multi-axis comparisons
- BarChart — vertical bars for the same categorical data
- HorizontalBarChart — horizontal bars with proportional fills
- ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive; PieChart implements its own inline tooltip


========================================
PixelArt
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web

Convert Unicode symbols or string art into Box-based geometry for Love2D compatibility

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

PixelArt converts Unicode symbols (▶ ⏸ ● ✓ arrows, etc.) and custom string art into rendered Box grids. Love2D's default font does not include Unicode symbols, so PixelArt renders them as colored Box elements instead. It provides a library of built-in symbols (play, pause, check, heart, etc.) and supports custom bitmap art defined as string arrays.

The component and hook use a "filled pixel" convention: any non-space character in the input string represents a filled pixel, which renders as a Box with the specified color. Spaces render as transparent. Per-row coloring is supported for multi-color designs like gradients or layered effects.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { PixelArt, usePixelArt, getPixelArtSymbols } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
// Component usage
<PixelArt art="play" size={4} color="#ffffff" />

// Hook usage
const playIcon = usePixelArt('play', { size: 4, color: '#ffffff' });
return <Box>{playIcon}</Box>;

// Get list of available symbols
const symbols = getPixelArtSymbols();
```

PixelArt Component Props:
| Prop   | Type              | Default     | Description                                    |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|------------------------------------------------|
| art    | string \| string[] | (required)  | Built-in symbol name OR array of string art    |
| size   | number            | 4           | Pixel size in logical units                    |
| color  | string            | '#ffffff'   | Single fill color for all pixels               |
| colors | string[]          | undefined   | Per-row fill colors (overrides color)          |
| gap    | number            | 0           | Gap between pixels in logical units            |
| style  | Style             | undefined   | Container style overrides                      |

usePixelArt Hook:
```tsx
function usePixelArt(
  art: string | string[],
  options?: PixelArtOptions
): React.ReactElement
```

Options:
| Key    | Type      | Default    | Description                              |
|--------|-----------|------------|------------------------------------------|
| size   | number    | 4          | Pixel size in logical units              |
| color  | string    | '#ffffff'  | Single fill color for all pixels         |
| colors | string[]  | undefined  | Per-row fill colors (overrides color)    |
| gap    | number    | 0          | Gap between pixels in logical units      |

getPixelArtSymbols():
Returns an array of all built-in symbol names.

Built-in Symbols:
play, pause, stop, skip-next, skip-prev, check, close, arrow-up, arrow-down, arrow-left, arrow-right, circle, circle-outline, dot, plus, minus, heart, star, volume, mute, chevron-right, chevron-left, menu

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Built-in play icon
--------
```tsx
import { PixelArt, Box } from '@reactjit/core';

function PlayButton() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ padding: 16, backgroundColor: '#313244', borderRadius: 8 }}>
      <PixelArt art="play" size={6} color="#a6e3a1" />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Custom string art (single color)
--------
```tsx
import { PixelArt } from '@reactjit/core';

function CustomIcon() {
  const logo = [
    ' ███ ',
    '█   █',
    '█ ▪ █',
    '█   █',
    ' ███ ',
  ];

  return <PixelArt art={logo} size={8} color="#f9e2af" />;
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Multi-color gradient heart
--------
```tsx
import { PixelArt } from '@reactjit/core';

function Heart() {
  const heartArt = [
    '  ███   ███  ',
    ' █████ █████ ',
    '█████████████',
    ' ███████████ ',
    '  █████████  ',
    '   ███████   ',
    '    █████    ',
    '     ███     ',
    '      █      ',
  ];

  const heartColors = [
    '#ff6b9d',
    '#ff5277',
    '#e94560',
    '#c9184a',
    '#a4133c',
    '#800f2f',
    '#590d22',
    '#3c0919',
    '#1a040c',
  ];

  return <PixelArt art={heartArt} size={12} colors={heartColors} />;
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Using the hook for dynamic icons
--------
```tsx
import { useState } from 'react';
import { usePixelArt, Box, Pressable } from '@reactjit/core';

function MediaControls() {
  const [isPlaying, setIsPlaying] = useState(false);

  const icon = usePixelArt(
    isPlaying ? 'pause' : 'play',
    { size: 5, color: isPlaying ? '#f38ba8' : '#a6e3a1' }
  );

  return (
    <Pressable onPress={() => setIsPlaying(!isPlaying)}>
      <Box style={{ padding: 12, backgroundColor: '#313244', borderRadius: 8 }}>
        {icon}
      </Box>
    </Pressable>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Icon grid with all built-in symbols
--------
```tsx
import { getPixelArtSymbols, PixelArt, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

function IconGallery() {
  const symbols = getPixelArtSymbols();

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', padding: 16, flexDirection: 'row', flexWrap: 'wrap', gap: 16 }}>
      {symbols.map(symbol => (
        <Box key={symbol} style={{ alignItems: 'center', gap: 4 }}>
          <Box style={{ padding: 8, backgroundColor: '#313244', borderRadius: 4 }}>
            <PixelArt art={symbol} size={4} color="#cdd6f4" />
          </Box>
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 10, color: '#a6adc8' }}>{symbol}</Text>
        </Box>
      ))}
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Animated arrow with pixel gap
--------
```tsx
import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';
import { PixelArt, Box } from '@reactjit/core';

function AnimatedArrow() {
  const [direction, setDirection] = useState<'arrow-up' | 'arrow-down' | 'arrow-left' | 'arrow-right'>('arrow-right');

  useEffect(() => {
    const directions = ['arrow-right', 'arrow-down', 'arrow-left', 'arrow-up'] as const;
    let index = 0;
    const interval = setInterval(() => {
      index = (index + 1) % 4;
      setDirection(directions[index]);
    }, 500);
    return () => clearInterval(interval);
  }, []);

  return (
    <Box style={{ padding: 24, backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e' }}>
      <PixelArt art={direction} size={8} color="#89dceb" gap={2} />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Custom navigation chevrons
--------
```tsx
import { PixelArt, Box, Text, Pressable } from '@reactjit/core';

function Pagination() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', flexDirection: 'row', justifyContent: 'space-between', padding: 16 }}>
      <Pressable onPress={() => console.log('prev')}>
        <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center', padding: 8 }}>
          <PixelArt art="chevron-left" size={3} color="#a6adc8" />
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Previous</Text>
        </Box>
      </Pressable>

      <Pressable onPress={() => console.log('next')}>
        <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center', padding: 8 }}>
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Next</Text>
          <PixelArt art="chevron-right" size={3} color="#a6adc8" />
        </Box>
      </Pressable>
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* **Never put Unicode symbols in `<Text>`** — Characters like ▶ ⏸ █ ● ✓ arrows, dingbats, and geometric shapes won't render in Love2D's default font. Always use PixelArt or Box-based geometry instead. The linter enforces this via `no-unicode-symbol-in-text`.
* **Filled pixel convention** — Any non-space character in the art string = filled pixel. Spaces = transparent. Use any character (█, X, #, etc.) for readability — they all render identically.
* **Size units are logical** — The `size` prop is in logical units, not pixels. On a 2x DPI screen, `size={4}` renders as 8 physical pixels.
* **Colors array falls back** — If `colors` array is shorter than the number of rows, remaining rows use the `color` prop. If neither is provided, defaults to white.
* **Memoization is automatic** — The `usePixelArt` hook memoizes the result. Changing `art`, `size`, `color`, `colors`, or `gap` triggers a re-render, but otherwise the element is cached.
* **Gap adds spacing** — The `gap` prop adds space between pixels, useful for retro/chunky aesthetics. Default is 0 (no gap).
* **Container style wraps** — When using the PixelArt component with a `style` prop, it wraps the rendered grid in an additional Box. When using the hook, no wrapper is added.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Box — The primitive used to render each pixel
- Layout rules (CLAUDE.md) — Critical rule #8: no Unicode symbols in Text
- Static linter — Enforces no-unicode-symbol-in-text at build time
- Storybook NeofetchDemo — Example of complex pixel art (heart logo)


========================================
Portal
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Render children at the top level of the tree, outside normal component hierarchy. Used for modals, dropdowns, tooltips, and overlays.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

Portal renders children at the top level of the render tree, escaping the normal parent-child hierarchy. This is essential for UI elements that need to appear above all other content, such as modals, tooltips, dropdowns, and context menus. PortalHost is the container that receives portal content in native mode. In web mode, Portal falls back to ReactDOM.createPortal when no PortalHost is present.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { Portal, PortalHost } from '@reactjit/core';
```

**Portal Props**

| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| children | ReactNode | required | Content to render at the top level |
| name | string | undefined | Optional identifier for the portal (auto-generated if omitted) |

**PortalHost Props**

| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| children | ReactNode | required | The main app content (renders below portals) |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Basic Portal Usage
--------
```tsx
import { Portal, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

function App() {
  const [showOverlay, setShowOverlay] = useState(false);

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
      <Text fontSize={16}>Main content</Text>
      <Pressable onPress={() => setShowOverlay(true)}>
        <Text fontSize={14}>Show Overlay</Text>
      </Pressable>

      {showOverlay && (
        <Portal>
          <Box
            style={{
              position: 'absolute',
              top: 0,
              left: 0,
              width: '100%',
              height: '100%',
              backgroundColor: [0, 0, 0, 0.7],
              justifyContent: 'center',
              alignItems: 'center',
            }}
          >
            <Box
              style={{
                width: 400,
                height: 200,
                backgroundColor: [1, 1, 1, 1],
                borderRadius: 8,
                padding: 20,
              }}
            >
              <Text fontSize={18}>Portal Overlay</Text>
              <Pressable onPress={() => setShowOverlay(false)}>
                <Text fontSize={14}>Close</Text>
              </Pressable>
            </Box>
          </Box>
        </Portal>
      )}
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

EXAMPLE: Setting Up PortalHost in Native Mode
--------
```tsx
import { PortalHost, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';
import { createRoot } from '@reactjit/native';
import { NativeBridge } from '@reactjit/native';
import { BridgeProvider, RendererProvider } from '@reactjit/core';

function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
      <Text fontSize={16}>App content here</Text>
      {/* Portals will render above this content */}
    </Box>
  );
}

// Entry point
const bridge = new NativeBridge();
const root = createRoot(bridge);

root.render(
  <BridgeProvider bridge={bridge}>
    <RendererProvider mode="native">
      <PortalHost>
        <App />
      </PortalHost>
    </RendererProvider>
  </BridgeProvider>
);
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

EXAMPLE: Named Portals
--------
```tsx
import { Portal, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

function NotificationSystem() {
  return (
    <Portal name="notifications">
      <Box
        style={{
          position: 'absolute',
          top: 20,
          right: 20,
          width: 300,
        }}
      >
        <Text fontSize={14}>Notification message</Text>
      </Box>
    </Portal>
  );
}

function TooltipSystem() {
  return (
    <Portal name="tooltips">
      <Box
        style={{
          position: 'absolute',
          top: 100,
          left: 100,
        }}
      >
        <Text fontSize={12}>Tooltip text</Text>
      </Box>
    </Portal>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

EXAMPLE: Portal with Modal Component
--------
```tsx
import { Modal, Box, Text, Pressable } from '@reactjit/core';

function ModalDemo() {
  const [visible, setVisible] = useState(false);

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
      <Pressable onPress={() => setVisible(true)}>
        <Text fontSize={16}>Open Modal</Text>
      </Pressable>

      {/* Modal uses Portal internally */}
      <Modal
        visible={visible}
        onRequestClose={() => setVisible(false)}
        animationType="fade"
        backdropDismiss={true}
      >
        <Box
          style={{
            width: 500,
            height: 300,
            backgroundColor: [1, 1, 1, 1],
            borderRadius: 8,
            padding: 20,
          }}
        >
          <Text fontSize={18}>Modal Content</Text>
        </Box>
      </Modal>
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

Love2D:
- Portals render with zIndex: 1000 in absolute positioning
- PortalHost must wrap your root component in native-main.tsx
- Multiple portals stack in registration order (later portals appear on top)
- Portal content is positioned absolutely relative to the viewport

Web:
- Falls back to ReactDOM.createPortal when no PortalHost is present
- Creates a fixed-position div appended to document.body
- PortalHost is optional in web mode but recommended for consistency
- Supports CSS transitions and transforms on portal content
Terminal / Grid targets:
- Portals render in absolute coordinates over the character grid
- Use explicit positioning (top, left) to place portal content
- PortalHost required in all grid-based targets
- Z-ordering follows registration order

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* In native mode (Love2D, terminal, grid targets), you MUST wrap your root component in PortalHost for portals to work
* Portal children should use absolute positioning (position: 'absolute') with explicit coordinates
* Portal content renders at zIndex: 1000 by default — override via style if needed
* Multiple portals stack in registration order (first registered = bottom, last = top)
* The name prop is optional but recommended for debugging and stable portal identity
* Portal content is NOT clipped by parent containers — it always renders at the top level
* In web mode without PortalHost, Portal automatically falls back to ReactDOM.createPortal
* Do not nest PortalHost components — only one PortalHost per app root
* Portal children receive the same dimensions as the viewport (width: '100%', height: '100%')
* Use Portal for overlays, modals, dropdowns, tooltips, context menus, and notifications
* Portal does NOT handle backdrop clicks or Escape key dismissal — use Modal component for that
* Portals persist until unmounted — conditional rendering ({showPortal && <Portal>...</Portal>}) controls visibility

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Modal — Full-screen overlay with backdrop and animations (uses Portal internally)
- ContextMenu — Right-click menu system (uses Portal internally)
- Pressable — For interactive elements inside portal content
- ScrollView — Can be used inside portal content for scrollable overlays
- useWindowDimensions — Get viewport size for centering portal content
- Box positioning — position: 'absolute', top, left, right, bottom for portal layout


========================================
Pressable
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web

Interaction component with press, long press, and hover state tracking

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

Pressable is a high-level interaction component that tracks pressed, hovered, and focused states. It wraps Box internally and provides callbacks for press, long press, hover, and focus events. Unlike Box's raw event handlers, Pressable manages interaction lifecycle: it fires onPress only on a clean release (not when a long press fires), supports configurable long press delay, and exposes interaction state to children via a render prop pattern.

Pressable supports both static and function-based style and children props. When passed a function, it receives a PressableState object with { pressed, hovered, focused } booleans, letting you build reactive UI without manual state management. It also supports hitSlop to expand the touch target beyond visible bounds, and integrates keyboard accessibility (Enter/Space triggers press).

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { Pressable } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
<Pressable
  onPress={(event) => { /* fired on clean tap/click */ }}
  onLongPress={(event) => { /* fired after delayLongPress ms */ }}
  style={(state) => ({
    padding: 12,
    backgroundColor: state.pressed ? '#45475a' : state.hovered ? '#313244' : '#1e1e2e',
    borderRadius: 8,
  })}
>
  {(state) => (
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: state.pressed ? '#89b4fa' : '#cdd6f4' }}>
      {state.pressed ? 'Pressing...' : 'Press me'}
    </Text>
  )}
</Pressable>
```

Props:
| Prop              | Type                                              | Default   | Description                                  |
|-------------------|----------------------------------------------------|-----------|----------------------------------------------|
| onPress           | (event: LoveEvent) => void                         | undefined | Fires on release if long press did not fire  |
| onLongPress       | (event: LoveEvent) => void                         | undefined | Fires after delayLongPress milliseconds      |
| onPressIn         | (event: LoveEvent) => void                         | undefined | Fires immediately when press begins          |
| onPressOut        | (event: LoveEvent) => void                         | undefined | Fires when press ends (release or cancel)    |
| onHoverIn         | (event: LoveEvent) => void                         | undefined | Fires when pointer enters                    |
| onHoverOut        | (event: LoveEvent) => void                         | undefined | Fires when pointer leaves                    |
| disabled          | boolean                                            | false     | Disables all interaction                     |
| delayLongPress    | number                                             | 500       | Milliseconds before long press fires         |
| style             | Style | (state: PressableState) => Style            | undefined | Static style or function of interaction state|
| children          | ReactNode | (state: PressableState) => ReactNode   | REQUIRED  | Static children or render prop               |
| hitSlop           | number | HitSlop                                    | undefined | Expand touch target (px)                     |
| accessibilityRole | string                                             | undefined | ARIA role (web: defaults to 'button')        |

PressableState:
```ts
interface PressableState {
  pressed: boolean;   // true while pointer is down
  hovered: boolean;   // true while pointer is over the element
  focused: boolean;   // true while keyboard-focused
}
```

HitSlop:
```ts
interface HitSlop {
  top?: number;
  bottom?: number;
  left?: number;
  right?: number;
}
```

When hitSlop is a number, it applies equally to all four sides.

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Simple button
--------
```tsx
<Pressable
  onPress={() => console.log('pressed')}
  style={{ padding: 12, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa', borderRadius: 8 }}
>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#1e1e2e' }}>Submit</Text>
</Pressable>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

EXAMPLE: Reactive style and children via render props
--------
```tsx
<Pressable
  onPress={() => setCount(c => c + 1)}
  style={(state) => ({
    padding: 16,
    backgroundColor: state.pressed ? '#585b70' : state.hovered ? '#45475a' : '#313244',
    borderRadius: 12,
    opacity: state.pressed ? 0.9 : 1,
  })}
>
  {(state) => (
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: state.hovered ? '#89b4fa' : '#cdd6f4' }}>
      {state.pressed ? 'Pressing...' : `Count: ${count}`}
    </Text>
  )}
</Pressable>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

EXAMPLE: Long press with expanded hit area
--------
```tsx
<Pressable
  onPress={() => console.log('tap')}
  onLongPress={() => console.log('long press!')}
  delayLongPress={800}
  hitSlop={20}
  style={{ padding: 8, backgroundColor: '#313244', borderRadius: 4 }}
>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 12, color: '#a6adc8' }}>Hold me</Text>
</Pressable>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

EXAMPLE: Disabled state
--------
```tsx
<Pressable
  onPress={() => {}}
  disabled={isLoading}
  style={{ padding: 12, backgroundColor: '#313244', borderRadius: 8 }}
>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#6c7086' }}>
    {isLoading ? 'Loading...' : 'Submit'}
  </Text>
</Pressable>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

Love2D:
In native mode, Pressable wraps a Box and passes interaction state and hitSlop as props to the host element. The Lua event system handles hit testing with hitSlop expansion. Long press timing runs in JavaScript via setTimeout.

Web:
Renders as a <div> with pointer event handlers, tabIndex for keyboard focus, and role attribute for accessibility. Disabled state sets cursor: not-allowed and opacity: 0.5. HitSlop is implemented via negative margin + positive padding trick to expand the clickable area without changing visual bounds. Keyboard accessibility: Enter and Space trigger press.

Terminal:
Not directly available on grid targets. Build equivalent interaction with the grid target's own event system.

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* onPress fires only on release and only if onLongPress did not fire during the same gesture. If you need to know immediately when the finger goes down, use onPressIn.
* The children prop is required. Pressable with no children renders an empty, invisible touch target.
* When disabled is true, all event handlers are suppressed and state resets to { pressed: false, hovered: false, focused: false }.
* If pointer leaves the element while pressed, the press is canceled (pressed becomes false, long press timer is cleared).
* hitSlop expands the interactive area, not the visual area. The element looks the same but responds to touches outside its visible bounds.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Box
- Modal
- Slider
- Switch


========================================
ProgressBar
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Progress indicator bar that fills proportionally from 0 to 1, with optional label, animation, and interactive hover tooltip

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

ProgressBar displays a horizontal fill bar representing progress from 0 to 1. The fill width is rendered as a percentage of the track width. An optional label shows the percentage or custom text overlay, and the `animated` prop uses `useSpring` to smoothly interpolate value changes.

The component uses Box and Text primitives, making it compatible with all targets. The track and fill both use pill-shaped border radii derived from the height. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering the bar reveals a ChartTooltip showing the percentage and exact fill fraction.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { ProgressBar } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Props:
| Prop        | Type    | Default    | Description                                                              |
|-------------|---------|------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| value       | number  | required   | Progress value from 0 to 1; clamped internally to [0, 1]                 |
| color       | Color   | '#3b82f6'  | Fill bar color                                                           |
| trackColor  | Color   | '#1e293b'  | Background track color                                                   |
| height      | number  | 8          | Bar height in pixels                                                     |
| showLabel   | boolean | false      | Show percentage label overlaid on the bar (requires height >= 14)        |
| label       | string  | undefined  | Custom label text; defaults to the computed percentage (e.g. "65%")     |
| animated    | boolean | false      | Animate value changes with a spring (stiffness: 120, damping: 20)       |
| interactive | boolean | false      | Enable hover to show a ChartTooltip with percentage and fill fraction    |
| style       | Style   | undefined  | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box                     |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Basic progress bar
--------
```tsx
import { ProgressBar } from '@reactjit/core';

export default function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <ProgressBar value={0.42} style={{ width: '100%' }} />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Progress bar with visible label
--------
```tsx
import { ProgressBar } from '@reactjit/core';

export default function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <ProgressBar
        value={0.75}
        showLabel
        height={20}
        color="#22c55e"
        style={{ width: 200 }}
      />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Animated progress with custom label and interactive tooltip
--------
```tsx
import { ProgressBar } from '@reactjit/core';

export default function App({ downloaded, total }: { downloaded: number; total: number }) {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <ProgressBar
        value={downloaded / total}
        animated
        label={`${downloaded} / ${total} MB`}
        showLabel
        height={18}
        color="#f59e0b"
        trackColor="#1e1e24"
        interactive
        style={{ width: '100%' }}
      />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The `value` prop is required and is clamped to [0, 1] internally. Values outside this range are silently clamped — no error is thrown.
* ProgressBar needs an explicit `width` either via the `style` prop or from its parent layout. Without width the track collapses to zero.
* The `showLabel` prop is silently ignored when `height < 14` — the text physically does not fit within the bar at that height.
* When `animated` is true, the fill width is driven by `useSpring` targeting `value * 100`. The hook is called unconditionally, so animated cannot be toggled conditionally at runtime.
* Label text is positioned absolutely and centered over the full track width. It is always white (`color: '#ffffff'`) with `fontWeight: 'bold'`.
* Label font size is `Math.max(9, height - 4)` — it scales with bar height but never goes below 9px.
* When both `label` and `showLabel` are provided, the custom `label` string is shown instead of the computed percentage.
* `interactive` adds a ChartTooltip showing the percentage and the raw clamped fraction (e.g. "0.75 / 1.00"). Hover requires pointer support.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Sparkline — compact inline trend visualization
- BarChart — multi-bar vertical chart for comparative data
- ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive used internally by this component


========================================
RadarChart
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Pixel-grid radar (spider) chart for comparing multi-axis performance profiles

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

RadarChart rasterizes a spider/radar chart into a 2x2-pixel cell grid. Each axis radiates from the center at equal angular intervals and data values are plotted along each axis, normalized either to a per-axis maximum or to the global maximum across all values. The region formed by connecting those points is filled with the chart color at 60% opacity. Concentric ring grid lines at 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% are rendered in a separate grid color. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering the chart shows a tooltip listing each axis label, its raw value, and the normalized percentage.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { RadarChart } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Data shape:
```tsx
interface RadarChartAxis {
  label: string;  // Axis name shown in the hover tooltip
  max?:  number;  // Per-axis maximum for normalization; defaults to global max across all data values
}
```

Props:
| Prop        | Type              | Default      | Description                                                                                   |
|-------------|-------------------|--------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| axes        | RadarChartAxis[]  | required     | Axis definitions; length must equal data.length                                               |
| data        | number[]          | required     | One raw value per axis, in the same order as axes                                             |
| size        | number            | 120          | Width and height of the chart square in pixels                                                |
| color       | Color             | '#3b82f6'    | Fill color for the data polygon (rendered at 60% opacity)                                     |
| gridColor   | Color             | '#1e293b'    | Color for concentric ring grid lines and axis spokes                                          |
| interactive | boolean           | false        | Enable hover to show the per-axis value breakdown tooltip                                     |
| style       | Style             | undefined    | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box                                          |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Player stats profile
--------
```tsx
import { RadarChart } from '@reactjit/core';

const axes = [
  { label: 'Speed',    max: 100 },
  { label: 'Strength', max: 100 },
  { label: 'Defense',  max: 100 },
  { label: 'Magic',    max: 100 },
  { label: 'Stamina',  max: 100 },
];

const stats = [78, 55, 62, 90, 70];

export default function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <RadarChart
        axes={axes}
        data={stats}
        size={160}
        color="#6366f1"
      />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: System health with per-axis maximums
--------
```tsx
import { RadarChart } from '@reactjit/core';

const axes = [
  { label: 'CPU',     max: 100  },
  { label: 'Memory',  max: 16   },
  { label: 'Disk',    max: 500  },
  { label: 'Network', max: 1000 },
  { label: 'IOPS',    max: 50   },
  { label: 'Load',    max: 8    },
];

const values = [72, 11.4, 380, 430, 34, 3.2];

export default function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <RadarChart
        axes={axes}
        data={values}
        size={200}
        color="#22c55e"
        gridColor="#0f172a"
      />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Interactive profile comparison widget
--------
```tsx
import { RadarChart } from '@reactjit/core';

const axes = [
  { label: 'Quality',    max: 10 },
  { label: 'Speed',      max: 10 },
  { label: 'Cost',       max: 10 },
  { label: 'Support',    max: 10 },
  { label: 'Scalability',max: 10 },
];

const scores = [8, 6, 7, 9, 5];

export default function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <RadarChart
        axes={axes}
        data={scores}
        size={180}
        color="#f59e0b"
        interactive
      />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* `axes` and `data` must have the same length. Extra data values beyond the axis count are ignored; missing data values default to 0 for normalization purposes.
* When a per-axis `max` is omitted, that axis is normalized against the global maximum of the entire `data` array. Mix explicit and implicit maxima with care — a single outlier value can compress all other axes.
* The rendered polygon fill uses 60% opacity (hardcoded). The `color` prop controls the fill color only; the opacity is not configurable.
* Grid ring lines are at 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% of the radius. They cannot be configured or hidden via props.
* The tooltip appears to the right of the chart (`left: '100%'`). Ensure the parent container has horizontal room, or the tooltip will be clipped.
* Rasterization cost scales with `size`. Prefer sizes in the 80–200px range.
* Axes are arranged clockwise starting from the top (12 o'clock position). The first axis in the `axes` array is always at the top.
* Do not use Unicode symbols in axis `label` strings.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- PieChart — another pixel-rasterized chart for proportional data
- BarChart — simpler vertical bars for comparing the same axes
- HorizontalBarChart — horizontal orientation for the same kind of multi-value comparison
- ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive; RadarChart implements its own inline tooltip


========================================
Radio / RadioGroup
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Radio button with RadioGroup for exclusive single-selection within a group.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

Radio and RadioGroup work together for exclusive selection. RadioGroup wraps a set of Radio components and provides shared context so that selecting one Radio automatically deselects the others.

RadioGroup supports both controlled and uncontrolled modes. In controlled mode, pass `value` and respond to `onValueChange`. In uncontrolled mode, optionally set `defaultValue`. Each Radio reads the group state from React context and renders a circular indicator that fills when its value matches the group's selected value.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Import: import { Radio, RadioGroup } from '@reactjit/core';

Usage:
```tsx
<RadioGroup value={selected} onValueChange={setSelected}>
  <Radio value="a" label="Option A" />
  <Radio value="b" label="Option B" />
  <Radio value="c" label="Option C" />
</RadioGroup>
```

RadioGroup Props:

| Prop          | Type                      | Default | Description                                |
|---------------|---------------------------|---------|--------------------------------------------|
| value         | string                    | -       | Controlled selected value                  |
| defaultValue  | string                    | -       | Initial value in uncontrolled mode         |
| onValueChange | (value: string) => void   | -       | Called when selection changes               |
| disabled      | boolean                   | false   | Disables all Radio children                |
| children      | ReactNode                 | -       | Radio components (required)                |
| style         | Style                     | -       | Container style overrides                  |

Radio Props:

| Prop           | Type    | Default    | Description                               |
|----------------|---------|------------|-------------------------------------------|
| value          | string  | (required) | Value this radio represents               |
| label          | string  | -          | Text label rendered beside the circle     |
| disabled       | boolean | false      | Disables this specific radio              |
| size           | number  | 20         | Circle diameter in pixels                 |
| color          | Color   | '#3b82f6'  | Border and inner dot color when selected  |
| uncheckedColor | Color   | '#6b7280'  | Border color when not selected            |
| style          | Style   | -          | Container style overrides                 |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Controlled radio group
--------
```tsx
const [plan, setPlan] = useState('free');

<RadioGroup value={plan} onValueChange={setPlan}>
  <Radio value="free" label="Free" />
  <Radio value="pro" label="Pro" />
  <Radio value="enterprise" label="Enterprise" />
</RadioGroup>
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Uncontrolled with custom styling
--------
```tsx
<RadioGroup defaultValue="dark">
  <Radio value="light" label="Light" color="#f59e0b" size={24} />
  <Radio value="dark" label="Dark" color="#f59e0b" size={24} />
</RadioGroup>
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Disabled group
--------
```tsx
<RadioGroup value="locked" disabled>
  <Radio value="locked" label="Locked option" />
  <Radio value="other" label="Other option" />
</RadioGroup>
```
--------
Platforms: all

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* Radio MUST be wrapped in a RadioGroup. A Radio rendered outside a RadioGroup will not respond to clicks.
* The `value` prop on Radio is required and must be a string. It identifies which option this Radio represents within the group.
* In controlled mode, the RadioGroup does not update its selection unless the parent updates the `value` prop in response to `onValueChange`.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Checkbox
- Switch
- Select


========================================
Scene
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d

A 3D viewport component that participates in the 2D layout system

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

Scene is the root container for all 3D rendering in ReactJIT. It creates an off-screen canvas that the Love2D painter composites into the 2D layout at the node's computed position. Children of Scene must be 3D elements: Camera, Mesh, AmbientLight, and DirectionalLight. On web targets, Scene renders a placeholder div because 3D support is Love2D only.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { Scene } from '@reactjit/3d';
```

Props:
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| style | Style | — | Style applied to the viewport box in the 2D layout (width, height, etc.) |
| backgroundColor | string | '#12121b' | Background color of the 3D viewport as a hex string |
| stars | boolean | — | Render procedural stars in the background |
| orbitControls | boolean | — | Enable Lua-side orbit controls — drag to rotate all meshes with zero latency |
| children | ReactNode | — | 3D child elements: Camera, Mesh, AmbientLight, DirectionalLight |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Minimal scene with a box mesh
--------
```tsx
import { Scene, Camera, Mesh } from '@reactjit/3d';

function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
      <Scene style={{ width: '100%', height: 400 }}>
        <Camera position={[0, -4, 2]} lookAt={[0, 0, 0]} />
        <Mesh geometry="box" color="#89b4fa" />
      </Scene>
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d

EXAMPLE: Scene with starfield background and orbit controls
--------
```tsx
import { Scene, Camera, Mesh, AmbientLight, DirectionalLight } from '@reactjit/3d';

function SpaceScene() {
  return (
    <Scene
      style={{ width: 800, height: 600 }}
      backgroundColor="#0a0a14"
      stars
      orbitControls
    >
      <Camera position={[0, -5, 3]} lookAt={[0, 0, 0]} fov={Math.PI / 3} />
      <AmbientLight color="#1a1a2e" intensity={0.15} />
      <DirectionalLight direction={[1, -1, 1]} color="#ffffff" intensity={1.0} />
      <Mesh geometry="sphere" texture="planet" seed={42} />
    </Scene>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d

EXAMPLE: Scene embedded in a 2D layout
--------
```tsx
import { Scene, Camera, Mesh, AmbientLight, DirectionalLight } from '@reactjit/3d';

function Dashboard() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', flexDirection: 'row' }}>
      <Box style={{ width: 300, height: '100%', backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e' }}>
        <Text fontSize={16}>Controls</Text>
      </Box>
      <Scene style={{ flexGrow: 1, height: '100%' }} backgroundColor="#12121b">
        <Camera position={[3, -3, 3]} lookAt={[0, 0, 0]} />
        <AmbientLight intensity={0.2} />
        <DirectionalLight direction={[1, -2, 1]} />
        <Mesh geometry="box" color="#cba6f7" position={[0, 0, 0]} />
      </Scene>
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* Scene must be given an explicit width and height via the style prop — it does not auto-size from its 3D children
* All direct children must be 3D elements (Camera, Mesh, AmbientLight, DirectionalLight); mixing 2D Box/Text children inside Scene is not supported
* Scene renders nothing useful on web targets — it shows a placeholder div with text
* Only one Scene can own a given region of the layout; nested Scenes are not supported
* orbitControls operates on the Lua side with zero JS round-trip latency; rotating via orbitControls does not fire React state updates

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Camera — defines the viewpoint inside a Scene
- Mesh — renderable 3D geometry or model inside a Scene
- AmbientLight — uniform base illumination inside a Scene
- DirectionalLight — sun-like directional light inside a Scene


========================================
ScrollView
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web

Scrollable container with imperative scrollTo API

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

ScrollView is a scrollable container that clips its children to its bounds and allows scrolling to reveal overflow content. It supports both vertical (default) and horizontal scrolling, optional scroll indicators, and scroll lifecycle callbacks. ScrollView also exposes an imperative scrollTo() method via React.forwardRef, letting you programmatically scroll to a specific position with optional animation.

In web mode, ScrollView renders as a `<div>` with overflow: auto and CSS-based scrolling. In native mode, it outputs a `'View'` host element with overflow: 'scroll', and the Lua layout engine handles scissor clipping, scroll translation, scrollbar rendering, and wheel event processing.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { ScrollView } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { ScrollViewRef, ScrollEvent } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
const scrollRef = React.useRef<ScrollViewRef>(null);

<ScrollView
  ref={scrollRef}
  style={{ width: '100%', height: 300 }}
  onScroll={(event) => console.log(event.scrollY)}
>
  {/* content taller than 300px */}
</ScrollView>
```

Props:
| Prop                | Type                             | Default   | Description                              |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| style               | Style                            | undefined | Container sizing and visual styling      |
| horizontal          | boolean                          | false     | Scroll horizontally instead of vertically|
| showScrollIndicator | boolean                          | true      | Show scrollbar indicator                 |
| onScroll            | (event: ScrollEvent) => void     | undefined | Fires during scrolling                   |
| onScrollBegin       | () => void                       | undefined | Fires when scrolling starts              |
| onScrollEnd         | () => void                       | undefined | Fires when scrolling stops               |
| children            | ReactNode                        | undefined | Scrollable content                       |

ScrollEvent:
```ts
interface ScrollEvent {
  scrollX: number;        // Current horizontal scroll offset
  scrollY: number;        // Current vertical scroll offset
  contentWidth: number;   // Total width of scrollable content
  contentHeight: number;  // Total height of scrollable content
}
```

ScrollViewRef (imperative API):
```ts
interface ScrollViewRef {
  scrollTo(options: {
    x?: number;        // Target horizontal offset
    y?: number;        // Target vertical offset
    animated?: boolean; // Smooth scroll animation (default: false)
  }): void;
}
```

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Basic vertical scroll
--------
```tsx
<ScrollView style={{ width: '100%', height: 200 }}>
  {Array.from({ length: 20 }, (_, i) => (
    <Box key={i} style={{ padding: 12, borderBottomWidth: 1, borderColor: '#313244' }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>{`Item ${i + 1}`}</Text>
    </Box>
  ))}
</ScrollView>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

EXAMPLE: Horizontal scroll
--------
```tsx
<ScrollView horizontal style={{ width: '100%', height: 100 }}>
  {Array.from({ length: 10 }, (_, i) => (
    <Box key={i} style={{ width: 120, height: 80, margin: 8, backgroundColor: '#313244', borderRadius: 8 }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 12, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>{`Card ${i + 1}`}</Text>
    </Box>
  ))}
</ScrollView>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

EXAMPLE: Imperative scrollTo
--------
```tsx
const scrollRef = React.useRef<ScrollViewRef>(null);

<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
  <Pressable onPress={() => scrollRef.current?.scrollTo({ y: 0, animated: true })}>
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#89b4fa' }}>Back to top</Text>
  </Pressable>

  <ScrollView ref={scrollRef} style={{ width: '100%', flexGrow: 1 }}>
    {/* long content */}
  </ScrollView>
</Box>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

EXAMPLE: Hidden scrollbar with scroll tracking
--------
```tsx
const [progress, setProgress] = useState(0);

<ScrollView
  style={{ width: '100%', height: 400 }}
  showScrollIndicator={false}
  onScroll={(e) => {
    const maxScroll = e.contentHeight - 400;
    setProgress(maxScroll > 0 ? e.scrollY / maxScroll : 0);
  }}
>
  {/* content */}
</ScrollView>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

Love2D:
The Lua layout engine implements scroll by translating child positions within a scissor-clipped region. Wheel events adjust scroll position. Scroll indicators are drawn as thin overlay rectangles by the painter. The imperative scrollTo() updates style.scrollX/scrollY which the layout engine reads on the next pass. Animated scrolling is handled natively in Lua.

Web:
Uses native browser scrolling via overflow: auto. Horizontal mode sets overflowX: auto and overflowY: hidden (and vice versa for vertical). showScrollIndicator: false hides the scrollbar using scrollbarWidth: 'none' (Firefox) and msOverflowStyle: 'none' (legacy Edge). Webkit/Blink scrollbar hiding requires CSS pseudo-element styling not achievable via inline styles alone. The imperative scrollTo() calls element.scrollTo() with behavior: 'smooth' when animated is true. onScrollEnd is detected via 150ms debounce after the last scroll event.

Terminal:
Not directly available on grid targets. Grid layouts are fixed-size and do not scroll. For large datasets on grid targets, use pagination or FlatList-style windowing.

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* ScrollView MUST have a constrained size (explicit width/height or parent constraints). Without a known size, there is nothing to overflow and nothing to scroll.
* Do not nest ScrollViews in the same axis. A vertical ScrollView inside another vertical ScrollView creates ambiguous scroll targets. Cross-axis nesting (horizontal inside vertical) is acceptable.
* When horizontal is true, the default flexDirection becomes 'row'. Children lay out left to right.
* Use FlatList instead of ScrollView for long lists of uniform items. ScrollView renders all children; FlatList virtualizes off-screen items for performance.
* The onScrollEnd callback fires after scrolling stops for 150ms. It is debounced, not instantaneous.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Box
- FlatList
- Layout System


========================================
Select
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Dropdown selection component with web-native select and native accordion-style expand/collapse.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

Select provides a dropdown-style selection from a list of options. Each option has a `label` (displayed) and a `value` (used programmatically).

Select supports both controlled and uncontrolled modes. In web mode, it renders a native HTML `<select>` element with custom styling. In native mode (Love2D), it renders an inline accordion: clicking the header toggles the option list open or closed, and clicking an option selects it and collapses the list.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Import: import { Select } from '@reactjit/core';

SelectOption type:
```ts
interface SelectOption {
  label: string;
  value: string;
}
```

Usage:
```tsx
<Select
  value={selected}
  onValueChange={setSelected}
  options={[
    { label: 'Red', value: 'red' },
    { label: 'Green', value: 'green' },
    { label: 'Blue', value: 'blue' },
  ]}
/>
```

Props:

| Prop          | Type                      | Default      | Description                               |
|---------------|---------------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------------|
| value         | string                    | -            | Controlled selected value                 |
| defaultValue  | string                    | -            | Initial value in uncontrolled mode        |
| onValueChange | (value: string) => void   | -            | Called when selection changes              |
| options       | SelectOption[]            | (required)   | Array of { label, value } options         |
| placeholder   | string                    | 'Select...'  | Text shown when no option is selected     |
| disabled      | boolean                   | false        | Disables interaction and dims opacity     |
| style         | Style                     | -            | Container style overrides                 |
| color         | Color                     | '#3b82f6'    | Accent color for open state border        |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Controlled select
--------
```tsx
const [lang, setLang] = useState<string>();

<Select
  value={lang}
  onValueChange={setLang}
  options={[
    { label: 'JavaScript', value: 'js' },
    { label: 'TypeScript', value: 'ts' },
    { label: 'Lua', value: 'lua' },
  ]}
  placeholder="Choose a language..."
/>
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Uncontrolled with default
--------
```tsx
<Select
  defaultValue="dark"
  options={[
    { label: 'Light', value: 'light' },
    { label: 'Dark', value: 'dark' },
    { label: 'System', value: 'system' },
  ]}
/>
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Disabled select
--------
```tsx
<Select
  value="locked"
  disabled
  options={[{ label: 'Locked', value: 'locked' }]}
/>
```
--------
Platforms: all

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The `options` prop is required. Passing an empty array renders a header with the placeholder but no options to select.
* In native mode, the open/close state is internal. There is no prop to force the dropdown open.
* When `disabled` is true, opacity is set to 0.5 and both the toggle and option clicks are ignored.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Radio
- Tabs
- Pressable


========================================
Slider
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web

Draggable range input with track and thumb for value selection

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

Slider provides a draggable input for selecting a numeric value within a range. It renders a track with an active fill and a draggable thumb. Slider supports both controlled (value prop) and uncontrolled (defaultValue prop) modes, step snapping, vertical orientation, and full visual customization of track and thumb colors and sizes.

In web mode, Slider handles drag via mouse events with global listeners for reliable tracking outside the element bounds. In native mode, it uses the Box drag event system (onDragStart, onDrag, onDragEnd) for pointer tracking. The native renderer uses a three-segment flex layout (active track, thumb, inactive track) rather than absolute positioning for compatibility with the Lua layout engine.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { Slider } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
const [volume, setVolume] = useState(0.5);

<Slider
  value={volume}
  onValueChange={setVolume}
  minimumValue={0}
  maximumValue={1}
  step={0.1}
  style={{ width: 200 }}
/>
```

Props:
| Prop             | Type                          | Default     | Description                                |
|------------------|-------------------------------|-------------|--------------------------------------------|
| value            | number                        | undefined   | Controlled value (overrides internal state)|
| defaultValue     | number                        | 0           | Initial value for uncontrolled mode        |
| onValueChange    | (value: number) => void       | undefined   | Called on every value change during drag    |
| onSlidingStart   | () => void                    | undefined   | Called when drag begins                    |
| onSlidingEnd     | (value: number) => void       | undefined   | Called when drag ends with final value     |
| minimumValue     | number                        | 0           | Minimum allowed value                      |
| maximumValue     | number                        | 1           | Maximum allowed value                      |
| step             | number                        | undefined   | Snap value to step increments              |
| disabled         | boolean                       | false       | Disable interaction                        |
| style            | Style                         | undefined   | Container style (width sets track length)  |
| trackColor       | Color                         | '#333333'   | Inactive track color                       |
| activeTrackColor | Color                         | '#4A90D9'   | Active (filled) track color                |
| thumbColor       | Color                         | '#ffffff'   | Thumb color                                |
| thumbSize        | number                        | 20          | Thumb diameter in pixels                   |
| trackHeight      | number                        | 4           | Track height in pixels                     |
| vertical         | boolean                       | false       | Vertical orientation                       |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Basic horizontal slider
--------
```tsx
const [value, setValue] = useState(50);

<Slider
  value={value}
  onValueChange={setValue}
  minimumValue={0}
  maximumValue={100}
  style={{ width: 300 }}
/>
<Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>{`Value: ${value}`}</Text>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

EXAMPLE: Stepped slider with custom colors
--------
```tsx
const [rating, setRating] = useState(3);

<Slider
  value={rating}
  onValueChange={setRating}
  minimumValue={1}
  maximumValue={5}
  step={1}
  trackColor="#45475a"
  activeTrackColor="#a6e3a1"
  thumbColor="#a6e3a1"
  thumbSize={24}
  trackHeight={6}
  style={{ width: 250 }}
/>
<Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>{`Rating: ${rating}/5`}</Text>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

EXAMPLE: Uncontrolled slider with callbacks
--------
```tsx
<Slider
  defaultValue={0.5}
  minimumValue={0}
  maximumValue={1}
  onSlidingStart={() => console.log('started')}
  onValueChange={(v) => console.log('current:', v)}
  onSlidingEnd={(v) => console.log('final:', v)}
  style={{ width: 200 }}
/>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

EXAMPLE: Vertical slider
--------
```tsx
const [level, setLevel] = useState(0.7);

<Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', alignItems: 'center', gap: 12, height: 200 }}>
  <Slider
    value={level}
    onValueChange={setLevel}
    vertical
    style={{ height: 180 }}
    activeTrackColor="#89b4fa"
  />
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>{`${Math.round(level * 100)}%`}</Text>
</Box>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

EXAMPLE: Disabled slider
--------
```tsx
<Slider
  value={0.3}
  disabled
  style={{ width: 200 }}
/>
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

Love2D:
Native mode uses a three-segment flex row layout: active track segment, thumb circle, inactive track segment. This avoids position: absolute which has limitations in the Lua layout engine for this use case. Drag tracking uses the Box drag event system (onDragStart, onDrag, onDragEnd) with totalDeltaX/totalDeltaY from the LoveEvent. Track width defaults to 200px if not specified via style.width. The value-at-drag-start is captured and deltas are applied relative to it for smooth, predictable dragging.

Web:
Uses absolute positioning for the active track fill and thumb within the track container. Mouse events use global document listeners (mousemove, mouseup) during drag for reliable tracking even when the cursor leaves the slider bounds. Disabled state shows cursor: not-allowed on the thumb.

Terminal:
Not available on grid targets. For value selection on character-grid renderers, build a custom component using keyboard input.

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* In controlled mode (value prop provided), you MUST update the value in onValueChange. If you do not, the thumb will not move because the component re-renders with the stale controlled value.
* The style.width prop (or style.height for vertical) determines the track length. Without it, the slider defaults to 200px. Always set an explicit size for predictable layout.
* step snapping rounds to the nearest step increment. A step of 0.1 with range 0-1 produces values: 0, 0.1, 0.2, ..., 1.0. The snapped value is clamped to [minimumValue, maximumValue].
* onValueChange fires on every drag movement (potentially many times per second). Do not perform expensive operations in this callback. Use onSlidingEnd for final-value operations like network requests or heavy state updates.
* onSlidingEnd fires once when the drag gesture completes. Its argument is the final value after step snapping and clamping.
* Disabled sliders render at 50% opacity and ignore all interaction.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Pressable
- Box
- Switch
- Style type reference


========================================
Spacer
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Flexible spacing component that fills available space or creates fixed gaps

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

Spacer is a layout utility component that creates space between elements. When used without props, it uses flexGrow to fill all available space in its parent container. When given a size prop, it creates a fixed-height gap. Spacer is the idiomatic way to push elements apart without manual margin calculations.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

import { Spacer } from '@reactjit/core';

Props:
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| size | number | undefined | Fixed height in pixels. If omitted, Spacer uses flexGrow: 1 to fill available space |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Push footer to bottom
--------
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', flexDirection: 'column' }}>
  <Text fontSize={24}>Header</Text>
  <Spacer />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Footer</Text>
</Box>
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Fixed gap between elements
--------
<Box style={{ flexDirection: 'column' }}>
  <Text fontSize={18}>First Item</Text>
  <Spacer size={20} />
  <Text fontSize={18}>Second Item</Text>
</Box>
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Multiple spacers distribute space
--------
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: 200, flexDirection: 'column' }}>
  <Text fontSize={16}>Top</Text>
  <Spacer />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Middle</Text>
  <Spacer />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Bottom</Text>
</Box>
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Horizontal spacing in row
--------
<Box style={{ width: '100%', flexDirection: 'row', alignItems: 'center' }}>
  <Text fontSize={16}>Left</Text>
  <Spacer />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Right</Text>
</Box>
--------
Platforms: all

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* Spacer with no size prop requires a parent with known dimensions — flexGrow only works when there's space to fill
* In row containers, Spacer fills horizontal space (width); in column containers, it fills vertical space (height)
* Fixed-size Spacers (with size prop) behave like Box with explicit height — they don't grow
* Never use Spacer inside auto-sized containers — flexGrow collapses to zero when there's no available space
* Multiple flexGrow Spacers in the same container share available space equally
* Prefer Spacer over manual marginTop/marginBottom for cleaner, more maintainable layouts
* Spacer is effectively a shorthand for Box with either flexGrow: 1 or a fixed height — use Box directly if you need additional styling

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- Box — primitive container that Spacer is built on
- Divider — visual separator line (vs. Spacer's invisible space)
- FlexRow/FlexColumn — helper components that often contain Spacers
- Layout Rules — understanding flexGrow behavior and container sizing


========================================
Sparkline
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Mini inline chart rendered as vertical bars normalized to the min/max of the data, with optional interactive hover tooltip

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

Sparkline renders a compact inline chart from an array of numbers. Each data point is represented as a thin vertical bar whose height is proportional to the value's position between the dataset's minimum and maximum. This creates a quick visual representation of trends or distributions within a small footprint.

Unlike BarChart, Sparkline is designed for inline use where labels and axes are unnecessary. It automatically computes bar widths to fill the given width and normalizes heights against the data range. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering a bar dims all others and shows a ChartTooltip with the raw value and index.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { Sparkline } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Props:
| Prop          | Type                                                    | Default    | Description                                                       |
|---------------|---------------------------------------------------------|------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|
| data          | number[]                                                | required   | Array of numeric data points                                      |
| width         | number                                                  | 80         | Total chart width in pixels                                       |
| height        | number                                                  | 24         | Total chart height in pixels                                      |
| color         | Color                                                   | '#3b82f6'  | Bar color                                                         |
| style         | Style                                                   | undefined  | Additional styles applied to the container Box                    |
| interactive   | boolean                                                 | false      | Enable hover highlighting and per-bar ChartTooltip                |
| onPointHover  | (index: number \| null, value: number \| null) => void  | undefined  | Called when the pointer enters or leaves a bar                    |
| onPointPress  | (index: number, value: number) => void                  | undefined  | Called when a bar is clicked (requires interactive: true)         |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Basic sparkline
--------
```tsx
import { Sparkline } from '@reactjit/core';

export default function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <Sparkline data={[10, 25, 15, 30, 20, 35, 28]} />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Custom size and color
--------
```tsx
import { Sparkline } from '@reactjit/core';

export default function App() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <Sparkline
        data={cpuHistory}
        width={120}
        height={32}
        color="#22c55e"
      />
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Inline with text and interactive hover callback
--------
```tsx
import { useState } from 'react';
import { Sparkline } from '@reactjit/core';

export default function App() {
  const [hovered, setHovered] = useState<number | null>(null);

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', alignItems: 'center', gap: 8 }}>
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 11, color: '#e2e8f0' }}>CPU</Text>
        <Sparkline
          data={cpuData}
          width={60}
          height={16}
          color="#ef4444"
          interactive
          onPointHover={(_, value) => setHovered(value)}
        />
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 11, color: '#94a3b8' }}>
          {hovered !== null ? `${hovered}%` : '72%'}
        </Text>
      </Box>
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The `data` prop is required. An empty array renders an empty placeholder Box at the specified width and height.
* Sparkline normalizes to the data's own min/max range, not 0-based. If all values are equal, the range defaults to 1, rendering all bars at minimum height (1px).
* The component has a fixed size based on `width` and `height`. It does not grow or shrink with its container. Pass `style` to override if needed.
* Bar widths are computed as `Math.max(1, (width - (data.length - 1) * barGap) / data.length)` where `barGap` is 1px. Very long data arrays in a narrow width may compress bars to 1px minimum.
* For real-time data, pass a new array reference to `data` on each update. Sparkline re-renders whenever the data reference changes.
* `onPointPress` only fires when `interactive` is also `true`.
* Hover events require pointer support — not available on all targets.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- BarChart — full-featured vertical bar chart with labels and axis context
- ProgressBar — single-value horizontal fill bar
- ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive used internally by this component


========================================
StackedBarChart
========================================

Category: Components | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Vertical stacked bar chart for visualizing multi-series data in proportional segments

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

StackedBarChart renders a column of vertical bars where each bar is subdivided into colored segments representing multiple data series. Series heights are proportional to the maximum column total across all bars, so every bar uses the full chart height at most. Optional column labels appear below the bars. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering a column dims all others and shows a tooltip with the column total and per-series breakdown.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { StackedBarChart } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Data shape:
```tsx
interface StackedBarChartSeries {
  label: string;   // Series name shown in the hover tooltip
  color: Color;    // Color for this series' segment
  data: number[];  // One value per bar column; must be the same length across all series
}
```

Props:
