SEE ALSO
---------

- useHotkey
- useLoveRPC


========================================
useDebug
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Register live component data in a global registry accessible from the inspector console.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useDebug registers arbitrary component data under a named key on `globalThis.__debug`, making it available for live evaluation in the Love2D inspector console (F12) or any JavaScript REPL attached to the runtime. Data is written on every render so values are always current, and the entry is automatically removed when the component unmounts. This hook adds zero visible output — it is purely a debugging side-channel, not an overlay or HUD.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { useDebug, getDebugData, registerDebug } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Hook signature:
```tsx
useDebug(key: string, data: any): void
```

Parameters:

| Parameter | Type   | Description                                                           |
|-----------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| key       | string | Unique identifier for the debug entry. Usually the component name.    |
| data      | any    | Any value to expose. Updated every render. Cleaned up on unmount.     |

Returns: void

Companion utilities:
```tsx
// Read all currently registered debug entries (non-hook, for tooling use).
getDebugData(): Record<string, any>

// Register debug data imperatively outside of React (e.g. in a game loop).
// Returns an unregister function — call it when done.
registerDebug(key: string, data: any): () => void
```

Console access patterns:
```
> __debug                         // all registered entries
> __debug['PlayerHUD']            // specific component
> __debug['PlayerHUD'].health     // specific field
:watch __debug['PlayerHUD'].fps   // live-updating watch expression
```

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Basic component state inspection
--------
```tsx
import { useDebug } from '@reactjit/core';

function PlayerHUD({ player }: { player: Player }) {
  const [health, setHealth] = useState(player.maxHealth);
  const [mana, setMana]     = useState(player.maxMana);

  useDebug('PlayerHUD', { health, mana, level: player.level });

  return (
    <Box>
      <Text fontSize={16}>HP: {health}</Text>
      <Text fontSize={16}>MP: {mana}</Text>
    </Box>
  );
}

// Inspector console (F12):
// > __debug['PlayerHUD']
// { health: 85, mana: 40, level: 12 }
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: FPS and frame counter via watch expression
--------
```tsx
import { useRef } from 'react';
import { useDebug } from '@reactjit/core';

function GameLoop({ deltaTime }: { deltaTime: number }) {
  const frameCount = useRef(0);
  frameCount.current++;

  const fps = deltaTime > 0 ? Math.round(1 / deltaTime) : 0;

  useDebug('GameLoop', { fps, frameCount: frameCount.current, deltaTime });

  return null;
}

// Inspector console:
// :watch __debug['GameLoop'].fps
// :watch __debug['GameLoop'].frameCount
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Multiple components sharing the debug registry
--------
```tsx
import { useDebug } from '@reactjit/core';

function Inventory({ items }: { items: Item[] }) {
  useDebug('Inventory', {
    count: items.length,
    totalWeight: items.reduce((s, i) => s + i.weight, 0),
  });
  return <Box>...</Box>;
}

function QuestLog({ quests }: { quests: Quest[] }) {
  useDebug('QuestLog', {
    active:    quests.filter(q => q.active).length,
    completed: quests.filter(q => q.completed).length,
  });
  return <Box>...</Box>;
}

// Inspector console:
// > __debug
// { Inventory: { count: 23, totalWeight: 45.2 }, QuestLog: { active: 3, completed: 17 } }
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Imperative registration from a game service
--------
```tsx
import { registerDebug } from '@reactjit/core';

const gameState = { running: true, paused: false, tickCount: 0 };

// Register once at startup; update the reference object in place each tick.
const unregister = registerDebug('GameState', gameState);

function tick() {
  gameState.tickCount++;
  // The object reference is already registered — mutations are visible immediately.
  requestAnimationFrame(tick);
}

tick();

// On teardown:
unregister();
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Read all debug data from tooling code
--------
```tsx
import { getDebugData } from '@reactjit/core';

function DebugSnapshot() {
  const snapshot = getDebugData();
  const keys = Object.keys(snapshot);

  return (
    <Box style={{ padding: 8, gap: 4 }}>
      {keys.map(k => (
        <Text key={k} fontSize={12}>{k}: {JSON.stringify(snapshot[k])}</Text>
      ))}
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- getDebugData — non-hook read of the full debug registry
- registerDebug — imperative registration for non-React code
- Inspector (F12) — Love2D dev overlay with console and watch expressions


========================================
useFetch
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Fetch JSON data from a URL or local file path with loading and error state.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useFetch wraps the global fetch() API in a React hook that manages loading, data, and error state. It works across all ReactJIT targets: on Love2D the fetch polyfill routes HTTP requests through LuaSocket and local file paths through love.filesystem; on web and Node targets the platform's native fetch is used. Pass null as the URL to clear state and skip any in-flight request, which is useful for conditional fetching. The options argument accepts standard RequestInit and is kept in a ref so changing it does not trigger a re-fetch.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { useFetch } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Parameters:
| Param   | Type           | Default   | Description |
|---------|----------------|-----------|-------------|
| url     | string \| null | required  | The URL or local file path to fetch. Pass null to skip fetching and clear state. |
| options | RequestInit    | undefined | Standard fetch options (method, headers, body, etc.). Changes to this object do not trigger a re-fetch. |

Returns:
| Field   | Type        | Description |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| data    | T \| null   | The parsed JSON response body, or null while loading or on error. |
| error   | Error \| null | The fetch or HTTP error, or null when successful or still loading. |
| loading | boolean     | True while the request is in flight. Starts as true when url is non-null. |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Fetch a list of users from an API
--------
```tsx
import { useFetch, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
}

function UserList() {
  const { data, loading, error } = useFetch<User[]>('https://api.example.com/users');

  if (loading) return <Text fontSize={14}>Loading...</Text>;
  if (error) return <Text fontSize={14}>Error: {error.message}</Text>;

  return (
    <Box flexDirection="column" gap={4} padding={12}>
      {data?.map(user => (
        <Text key={user.id} fontSize={14}>{user.name}</Text>
      ))}
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Load a local config file bundled with the Love2D project
--------
```tsx
import { useFetch, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

interface Config {
  version: string;
  theme: string;
}

function ConfigDisplay() {
  const { data: config, loading } = useFetch<Config>('data/config.json');

  if (loading || !config) return <Text fontSize={14}>Loading config...</Text>;

  return (
    <Box flexDirection="column" gap={4} padding={8}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Version: {config.version}</Text>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Theme: {config.theme}</Text>
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d

EXAMPLE: Conditional fetch — only fetch when an ID is selected
--------
```tsx
import { useState } from 'react';
import { useFetch, Box, Text, Pressable } from '@reactjit/core';

interface Item {
  id: number;
  title: string;
}

function ItemViewer() {
  const [selectedId, setSelectedId] = useState<number | null>(null);
  const url = selectedId != null ? `https://api.example.com/items/${selectedId}` : null;
  const { data, loading } = useFetch<Item>(url);

  return (
    <Box flexDirection="column" gap={8} padding={12} width="100%">
      <Box flexDirection="row" gap={8} width="100%">
        {[1, 2, 3].map(id => (
          <Pressable key={id} onPress={() => setSelectedId(id)}>
            <Box backgroundColor="#333" padding={8}>
              <Text fontSize={13}>Item {id}</Text>
            </Box>
          </Pressable>
        ))}
      </Box>
      {loading && <Text fontSize={14}>Loading...</Text>}
      {data && <Text fontSize={15}>{data.title}</Text>}
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: POST request with custom options
--------
```tsx
import { useFetch, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

interface CreateResponse {
  id: number;
}

function Creator() {
  const { data, loading, error } = useFetch<CreateResponse>(
    'https://api.example.com/items',
    {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify({ name: 'New Item' }),
    }
  );

  if (loading) return <Text fontSize={14}>Creating...</Text>;
  if (error) return <Text fontSize={14}>Failed: {error.message}</Text>;
  if (data) return <Text fontSize={14}>Created with id: {data.id}</Text>;
  return null;
}
```
--------
Platforms: web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- useWebSocket
- useLoveRPC


========================================
useHotkey
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Register a global keyboard shortcut that fires a handler when a key combo is pressed.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useHotkey binds a callback to a keyboard combination string such as "ctrl+z" or "ctrl+shift+s". The combo is parsed once on mount and matched against every keydown event received from the bridge. The handler reference is always kept current without re-subscribing, so passing an inline arrow function is safe. Pass `enabled: false` in options to conditionally disable the binding without unmounting the component.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { useHotkey } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Parameters:
| Param   | Type                       | Default   | Description |
|---------|----------------------------|-----------|-------------|
| combo   | string                     | required  | Key combination string. Use "+" as separator. Modifiers: ctrl, control, shift, alt, meta, cmd, gui. Key names are lowercased (e.g. "s", "z", "escape", "f1"). |
| handler | (event: LoveEvent) => void | required  | Called when the combo is matched. Receives the raw LoveEvent payload. |
| options | { enabled?: boolean }      | undefined | Optional config object. Set enabled: false to disable the binding temporarily. |

Returns:
void — this hook has no return value.

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Undo and redo shortcuts
--------
```tsx
import { useHotkey } from '@reactjit/core';

function Editor() {
  useHotkey('ctrl+z', () => undo());
  useHotkey('ctrl+shift+z', () => redo());

  return <EditorCanvas />;
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Escape closes a modal only while it is open
--------
```tsx
import { useState } from 'react';
import { useHotkey, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

function App() {
  const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);

  useHotkey('escape', () => setOpen(false), { enabled: open });

  return (
    <Box width="100%" height="100%">
      {open && (
        <Box backgroundColor="#222" padding={16}>
          <Text fontSize={14}>Press Escape to close</Text>
        </Box>
      )}
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Save with Ctrl+S and Ctrl+Shift+S for save-as
--------
```tsx
import { useHotkey } from '@reactjit/core';

function Document() {
  useHotkey('ctrl+s', () => save());
  useHotkey('ctrl+shift+s', () => saveAs());

  return <DocumentView />;
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Access the raw LoveEvent in the handler
--------
```tsx
import { useHotkey } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { LoveEvent } from '@reactjit/core';

function DevTools() {
  useHotkey('f12', (event: LoveEvent) => {
    console.log('key:', event.key, 'shift:', event.shift);
    toggleDebugPanel();
  });

  return <App />;
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d, web

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- useLoveEvent
- useClipboard


========================================
useLove
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Subscribe to Love2D events and get a send function for bidirectional communication.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useLove is the primary hook for subscribing to events from the Lua runtime and sending commands back to it. It returns a tuple of [state, send], where state is automatically updated whenever an event of the given type is received, and send is a function for dispatching commands to the Lua side.

The hook maintains a React state that updates whenever the bridge receives an event matching the specified event type. The initial state is provided as the second argument and is used before any events arrive.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Signature:
  function useLove<T>(eventType: string, initialState: T): [T, (cmd: string, payload?: any) => void]

Parameters:
  eventType: string
    The event type to subscribe to. Must match the event type dispatched by the Lua side
    (e.g., 'game:state', 'player:health', 'inventory:update').

  initialState: T
    The initial value for the state before any events are received.

Returns:
  [state, send]

  state: T
    The current state, updated whenever an event of the given type is received. The event
    payload replaces the state entirely.

  send: (cmd: string, payload?: any) => void
    Function to send commands to the Lua side. The cmd string identifies the command type,
    and the optional payload provides additional data.

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Track game state
--------
import { useLove } from '@reactjit/core';

function GameHUD() {
  const [gameState, send] = useLove('game:state', { score: 0, level: 1, lives: 3 });

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 16 }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 24, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>{`Score: ${gameState.score}`}</Text>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 18, color: '#a6e3a1' }}>{`Level: ${gameState.level}`}</Text>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 18, color: '#f38ba8' }}>{`Lives: ${gameState.lives}`}</Text>
      <Box style={{ width: 120, height: 40, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa', borderRadius: 8 }}
           onClick={() => send('game:restart')}>
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#1e1e2e' }}>Restart</Text>
      </Box>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Player movement controls
--------
import { useLove } from '@reactjit/core';

function Controls() {
  const [position, send] = useLove('player:position', { x: 0, y: 0 });

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>
        {`Position: ${position.x}, ${position.y}`}
      </Text>
      <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', width: '100%', gap: 8 }}>
        <Box style={{ width: 60, height: 60, backgroundColor: '#313244' }}
             onClick={() => send('player:move', { x: -10, y: 0 })}>
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 20, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Left</Text>
        </Box>
        <Box style={{ width: 60, height: 60, backgroundColor: '#313244' }}
             onClick={() => send('player:move', { x: 10, y: 0 })}>
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 20, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Right</Text>
        </Box>
      </Box>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

differences:
- Love2D: Events arrive via QuickJS FFI bridge. Send dispatches via the same bridge.
- Web: Events arrive via in-memory queue (Module.FS bridge). Send writes to the queue.
- Grid targets: Events arrive via IPC/WebSocket. Send dispatches via the same channel.

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The event payload replaces the state entirely -- it is not merged
* The send function is memoized (stable reference) and safe to use in callbacks
* The hook re-subscribes when eventType changes -- use a stable string, not a computed value
* Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
* The event type string must match exactly what the Lua side dispatches

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- useLoveEvent -- Fire-and-forget event listener (no state tracking)
- useLoveState -- Bidirectional shared state with automatic sync
- useLoveSend -- Just the send function without event subscription


========================================
useLoveEvent
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Fire-and-forget event listener for Love2D events without state tracking.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useLoveEvent subscribes to events from the Lua runtime and calls a handler function when events arrive. Unlike useLove, it does not track state -- it is a fire-and-forget listener for side effects like logging, triggering animations, playing sounds, or updating external state.

The hook uses a ref internally to store the handler function, which means the subscription does not re-register when the handler reference changes. This avoids unnecessary unsubscribe/resubscribe cycles when the handler is an inline function.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Signature:
  function useLoveEvent(eventType: string, handler: (payload: any) => void): void

Parameters:
  eventType: string
    The event type to listen for. Must match the event type dispatched by the Lua side.

  handler: (payload: any) => void
    Callback invoked when an event of the given type is received. The handler receives
    the event payload as its argument. Because the handler is stored in a ref, the
    latest handler is always called, even if the component re-renders with a new function.

Returns:
  void (no return value)

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Log spawned entities
--------
import { useLoveEvent } from '@reactjit/core';

function SpawnLogger() {
  useLoveEvent('entity:spawned', (data) => {
    console.log('Entity spawned:', data.type, 'at', data.x, data.y);
  });

  return null; // No UI -- just a listener
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Trigger animation on event
--------
import { useLoveEvent, useAnimation } from '@reactjit/core';

function DamageFlash() {
  const [flashAnim, flashValue] = useAnimation(0);

  useLoveEvent('player:damaged', (data) => {
    flashAnim.setValue(1);
    flashAnim.timing({ toValue: 0, duration: 500 }).start();
  });

  return (
    <Box style={{
      width: '100%',
      height: '100%',
      backgroundColor: `rgba(255, 0, 0, ${flashValue * 0.3})`,
    }} />
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Update external state
--------
import { useState } from 'react';
import { useLoveEvent } from '@reactjit/core';

function NotificationBanner() {
  const [message, setMessage] = useState('');

  useLoveEvent('notification', (data) => {
    setMessage(data.text);
    setTimeout(() => setMessage(''), 3000);
  });

  if (!message) return null;

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', padding: 12, backgroundColor: '#313244' }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#f9e2af' }}>{message}</Text>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The handler is stored in a ref -- always calls the latest version, no re-subscription on change
* The subscription is re-created only when eventType or the bridge instance changes
* This hook does NOT return state -- if you need to track the latest event value, use useLove instead
* Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
* The handler should be fast -- it runs synchronously when the event arrives

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- uselove.txt -- Event subscription with state tracking
- uselovesend.txt -- Send commands without subscribing to events


========================================
useLoveOverlays
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: advanced | Platforms: web

Position DOM overlays relative to Love2D entity coordinates in web mode.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useLoveOverlays positions DOM overlay elements based on Love2D entity coordinates received from the Lua runtime. This hook is only meaningful in web mode, where React controls both the Love2D canvas and surrounding DOM elements. It maps normalized coordinates (0-1) from the Lua runtime to pixel positions on the canvas DOM element.

The hook listens for 'overlays' events from the bridge, which provide a list of entities with normalized coordinates. It uses a ResizeObserver on the canvas element to track its size and position, then computes DOM pixel coordinates for each overlay.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Signature:
  function useLoveOverlays(canvasRef: React.RefObject<HTMLCanvasElement | null>): Overlay[]

Parameters:
  canvasRef: React.RefObject<HTMLCanvasElement | null>
    A React ref to the canvas element that Love2D renders into. Used to get the canvas
    bounding rectangle for coordinate mapping.

Returns:
  Overlay[]

Overlay interface:
  interface Overlay {
    id: string        // Unique identifier for the overlay entity
    nx: number        // Normalized X coordinate (0-1, from Lua)
    ny: number        // Normalized Y coordinate (0-1, from Lua)
    px: number        // Pixel X coordinate in game space (from Lua)
    py: number        // Pixel Y coordinate in game space (from Lua)
    domX?: number     // Computed DOM X pixel position on the canvas
    domY?: number     // Computed DOM Y pixel position on the canvas
    [key: string]: any  // Additional data from the Lua side
  }

Coordinate mapping:
  domX = nx * canvasRect.width
  domY = ny * canvasRect.height

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Render name labels above entities
--------
import { useRef } from 'react';
import { useLoveOverlays, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

function GameOverlays() {
  const canvasRef = useRef<HTMLCanvasElement>(null);
  const overlays = useLoveOverlays(canvasRef);

  return (
    <div style={{ position: 'relative' }}>
      <canvas ref={canvasRef} width={1280} height={720} />
      {overlays.map((overlay) => (
        <div
          key={overlay.id}
          style={{
            position: 'absolute',
            left: overlay.domX,
            top: overlay.domY,
            transform: 'translate(-50%, -100%)',
            pointerEvents: 'none',
          }}
        >
          <span style={{ color: 'white', fontSize: 12 }}>
            {overlay.name || overlay.id}
          </span>
        </div>
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: web

EXAMPLE: Tooltip on hover
--------
import { useRef, useState } from 'react';
import { useLoveOverlays } from '@reactjit/core';

function EntityTooltips() {
  const canvasRef = useRef<HTMLCanvasElement>(null);
  const overlays = useLoveOverlays(canvasRef);
  const [hovered, setHovered] = useState<string | null>(null);

  return (
    <div style={{ position: 'relative' }}>
      <canvas ref={canvasRef} width={1280} height={720} />
      {overlays.map((o) => (
        <div
          key={o.id}
          style={{
            position: 'absolute',
            left: o.domX,
            top: o.domY,
            width: 32,
            height: 32,
            transform: 'translate(-50%, -50%)',
          }}
          onMouseEnter={() => setHovered(o.id)}
          onMouseLeave={() => setHovered(null)}
        >
          {hovered === o.id && (
            <div style={{ background: 'rgba(0,0,0,0.8)', color: 'white', padding: 4, fontSize: 10, borderRadius: 4, whiteSpace: 'nowrap' }}>
              {`HP: ${o.health} | Lvl: ${o.level}`}
            </div>
          )}
        </div>
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: web

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* Only use in web mode -- has no effect on other platforms
* The canvasRef must point to the actual <canvas> element Love2D renders into
* Overlays are computed from 'overlays' events dispatched by the Lua side
* If the canvas ref is null or not yet mounted, the hook returns an empty array
* Overlay positions are recomputed when the canvas resizes (via ResizeObserver)
* Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- uselove.txt -- General event subscription
- useLoveEvent -- Fire-and-forget event handling


========================================
useLoveReady
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Detect when the Love2D bridge is initialized and ready for communication.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useLoveReady returns a boolean indicating whether the Love2D bridge is initialized and ready for communication. This is useful for showing loading states or deferring operations that depend on the bridge being available.

The hook checks the bridge's isReady() method on initial render. If the bridge is not yet ready, it registers an onReady callback that flips the state to true once initialization completes.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Signature:
  function useLoveReady(): boolean

Parameters:
  (none)

Returns:
  boolean
    true when the bridge is initialized and ready for communication.
    false while the bridge is still initializing.

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Loading screen
--------
import { useLoveReady } from '@reactjit/core';

function App() {
  const ready = useLoveReady();

  if (!ready) {
    return (
      <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', justifyContent: 'center', alignItems: 'center' }}>
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 24, color: '#a6adc8' }}>Loading...</Text>
      </Box>
    );
  }

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 20, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Ready!</Text>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Defer data fetching until ready
--------
import { useEffect } from 'react';
import { useLoveReady, useLoveRPC } from '@reactjit/core';

function DataLoader() {
  const ready = useLoveReady();
  const loadData = useLoveRPC('loadInitialData');

  useEffect(() => {
    if (ready) {
      loadData().then((data) => console.log('Data loaded:', data));
    }
  }, [ready, loadData]);

  return null;
}
--------
Platforms: all

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

The readiness condition depends on the target:
- Love2D: Ready when the QuickJS FFI bridge has established communication with the Lua runtime.
- Web: Ready when the Module.FS bridge has initialized its event queue.
- Grid targets: Ready when the IPC/WebSocket connection is established.
In most cases, the bridge becomes ready very quickly (within the first frame). The loading
state is most visible when the bridge requires asynchronous initialization (e.g., WebSocket
connection).

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The hook returns false synchronously on first render if the bridge is still initializing
* Once ready becomes true, it never reverts to false
* Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
* Do not send commands or subscribe to events before the bridge is ready -- use this hook to gate those operations

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- uselove.txt -- Event subscription (works only after ready)
- useloverpc.txt -- RPC calls (works only after ready)
- uselovesend.txt -- Send commands (works only after ready)


========================================
useLoveRPC
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Call Lua RPC methods and await results from the React side.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useLoveRPC provides a way to call Lua-side RPC (Remote Procedure Call) methods from React and await the results. Unlike the event-based hooks (useLove, useLoveEvent), this hook supports request-response patterns where you need a return value from the Lua runtime.

The hook returns a memoized async function. When called, it sends the RPC request via the bridge, waits for the response, and resolves the promise with the result. An optional timeout prevents hanging on unresponsive calls.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Signature:
  function useLoveRPC<T = any>(method: string): (args?: any, timeoutMs?: number) => Promise<T>

Type parameter:
  T    The expected return type of the RPC call. Defaults to any.

Parameters:
  method: string
    The name of the Lua-side RPC method to call. Must be registered on the Lua side.

Returns:
  (args?: any, timeoutMs?: number) => Promise<T>

  An async function that:
    args?: any         Optional arguments passed to the Lua method.
    timeoutMs?: number Optional timeout in milliseconds. If the Lua side does not respond
                       within this window, the promise rejects.

  Resolves with the return value from the Lua method (typed as T).

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Query nearby entities
--------
import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';
import { useLoveRPC } from '@reactjit/core';

interface Entity {
  id: number;
  type: string;
  x: number;
  y: number;
}

function NearbyList() {
  const getNearby = useLoveRPC<Entity[]>('getNearby');
  const [entities, setEntities] = useState<Entity[]>([]);

  useEffect(() => {
    const fetch = async () => {
      const nearby = await getNearby({ x: 100, y: 200, range: 500 });
      setEntities(nearby);
    };
    fetch();
  }, [getNearby]);

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 16 }}>
      {entities.map((e) => (
        <Text key={e.id} style={{ fontSize: 12, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>
          {`${e.type} at (${e.x}, ${e.y})`}
        </Text>
      ))}
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: RPC with timeout
--------
import { useLoveRPC } from '@reactjit/core';

function SaveButton() {
  const saveGame = useLoveRPC<boolean>('saveGame');

  const handleSave = async () => {
    try {
      const success = await saveGame({ slot: 1 }, 5000); // 5 second timeout
      console.log(success ? 'Saved!' : 'Save failed');
    } catch (err) {
      console.error('Save timed out or failed:', err);
    }
  };

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: 120, height: 40, backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1', borderRadius: 8 }}
         onClick={handleSave}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#1e1e2e' }}>Save</Text>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

target:
- Love2D: RPC call is dispatched via QuickJS FFI. The Lua side processes the call and sends
the response back through the bridge.
- Web: RPC is processed via the in-memory bridge queue.
- Grid targets: RPC travels over IPC/WebSocket.
The Lua-side RPC method must be registered to handle the call. Calling an unregistered
method will result in an error or timeout.

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The returned function is memoized (stable reference) -- safe to include in dependency arrays
* Always handle promise rejections (timeouts, errors from Lua side)
* The RPC method name must match a registered handler on the Lua side
* RPC is async -- do not use it in render functions, use it in effects or event handlers
* Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
* Large payloads may impact performance due to serialization overhead

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- uselove.txt -- Event-based communication (no request-response)
- uselovesend.txt -- Fire-and-forget commands (no response)
- useLoveState -- Shared state (auto-sync, no manual RPC)


========================================
useLoveSend
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Returns a fire-and-forget send function for dispatching commands to the Lua runtime.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useLoveSend returns a send function for dispatching fire-and-forget commands to the Lua runtime. Unlike useLove (which also subscribes to events and tracks state), this hook only provides the send capability. It is the lightest-weight hook for components that only need to send commands without receiving responses.

The returned function is memoized (stable reference), making it safe to include in dependency arrays and pass to child components.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Signature:
  function useLoveSend(): (type: string, payload?: any) => void

Parameters:
  (none)

Returns:
  (type: string, payload?: any) => void

  A function that sends a command to the Lua runtime:
    type: string       The command type identifier.
    payload?: any      Optional data payload sent with the command.

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Send a command on click
--------
import { useLoveSend } from '@reactjit/core';

function FireButton() {
  const send = useLoveSend();

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: 120, height: 50, backgroundColor: '#f38ba8', borderRadius: 8 }}
         onClick={() => send('weapon:fire', { x: 100, y: 200 })}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 18, color: '#1e1e2e' }}>Fire!</Text>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Pass send to child components
--------
import { useLoveSend } from '@reactjit/core';

function Toolbar() {
  const send = useLoveSend();

  return (
    <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', width: '100%', gap: 8, padding: 8 }}>
      <ActionButton label="Save" onPress={() => send('game:save')} />
      <ActionButton label="Load" onPress={() => send('game:load')} />
      <ActionButton label="Quit" onPress={() => send('game:quit')} />
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The returned send function is memoized -- safe for dependency arrays
* Fire-and-forget: no confirmation or response. Use useLoveRPC for request-response patterns
* Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
* Commands are processed asynchronously by the Lua runtime on the next update cycle

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- uselove.txt -- Send + event subscription + state tracking
- useloverpc.txt -- Send with response (request-response pattern)
- useloveevent.txt -- Listen for events without sending


========================================
useLoveState
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Bidirectional shared state between React and Lua with automatic synchronization.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useLoveState provides bidirectional shared state between React and the Lua runtime. It works like React's useState but with automatic synchronization: when you call setValue from React, it updates both the local React state and the Lua-side state via the bridge. When the Lua side updates the state, the change propagates to React via a `state:${key}` event.

This hook is ideal for settings, configuration values, and any state that both React and Lua need to read and write.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Signature:
  function useLoveState<T>(key: string, initialValue: T): [T, (value: T) => void]

Parameters:
  key: string
    A unique key identifying this shared state. The hook subscribes to `state:${key}` events
    from the bridge. Use dot notation for namespacing (e.g., 'player.health', 'settings.volume').

  initialValue: T
    The initial value before any sync events arrive from the Lua side.

Returns:
  [value, setValue]

  value: T
    The current value of the shared state.

  setValue: (value: T) => void
    Updates both the local React state and the Lua-side state. Calls setLocal(value) for
    immediate React update and bridge.setState(key, value) to sync to Lua.

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Shared volume setting
--------
import { useLoveState } from '@reactjit/core';

function VolumeControl() {
  const [volume, setVolume] = useLoveState('settings.volume', 0.8);

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', padding: 16 }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>
        {`Volume: ${Math.round(volume * 100)}%`}
      </Text>
      <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', width: '100%', gap: 8 }}>
        <Box style={{ width: 60, height: 40, backgroundColor: '#f38ba8', borderRadius: 4 }}
             onClick={() => setVolume(Math.max(0, volume - 0.1))}>
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#1e1e2e' }}>-</Text>
        </Box>
        <Box style={{ width: 60, height: 40, backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1', borderRadius: 4 }}
             onClick={() => setVolume(Math.min(1, volume + 0.1))}>
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#1e1e2e' }}>+</Text>
        </Box>
      </Box>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Player health synced from Lua
--------
import { useLoveState } from '@reactjit/core';

function HealthBar() {
  const [health, setHealth] = useLoveState('player.health', 100);

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: 200, height: 20, backgroundColor: '#313244', borderRadius: 4 }}>
      <Box style={{
        width: `${health}%`,
        height: '100%',
        backgroundColor: health > 50 ? '#a6e3a1' : '#f38ba8',
        borderRadius: 4,
      }} />
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

The bidirectional sync works on all platforms via the bridge abstraction:
- React to Lua: setValue calls bridge.setState(key, value)
- Lua to React: Lua dispatches a `state:${key}` event, which the hook subscribes to
The sync is eventual -- there may be a frame of latency between setValue and the Lua side
seeing the update, and vice versa. For time-critical state, consider using RPC instead.

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* setValue updates React state immediately (optimistic) and then syncs to Lua
* State from Lua arrives as events with type `state:${key}` -- the key must match exactly
* The setValue function is memoized (stable reference) when the key does not change
* Multiple components can share the same key -- they will all receive updates
* Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
* The initial value is used only on first render -- Lua-side state takes precedence once synced

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- uselove.txt -- Event subscription with manual send
- useloverpc.txt -- Request-response communication
- uselovesend.txt -- Fire-and-forget commands


========================================
usePeerServer
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: advanced | Platforms: love2d

Host a WebSocket server to accept peer connections, broadcast messages, and receive incoming data.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

usePeerServer starts a WebSocket server bound to the given port and manages the full lifecycle of peer connections: tracking connected client IDs, exposing the last received message with its sender ID, and providing send and broadcast functions. It routes through the bridge's __wsListen global, which is provided by the Love2D native target. The hook tears down the server automatically when the component unmounts or the port changes to a new value. Pass null as the port to keep the server stopped.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { usePeerServer } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { PeerMessage } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Parameters:
| Param | Type           | Default  | Description |
|-------|----------------|----------|-------------|
| port  | number \| null | required | TCP port to listen on (e.g. 8080). Pass null to keep the server stopped. |

Returns:
| Field       | Type                             | Description |
|-------------|----------------------------------|-------------|
| ready       | boolean                          | True once the server is bound and listening for connections. |
| peers       | number[]                         | Array of currently connected client IDs. Updated on connect and disconnect. |
| broadcast   | (data: string) => void           | Send a string message to every connected peer simultaneously. |
| send        | (clientId: number, data: string) => void | Send a string message to a single peer identified by its numeric client ID. |
| lastMessage | PeerMessage \| null              | The most recently received message, or null if nothing has arrived yet. |
| error       | string \| null                   | Human-readable error string if the server failed to start, otherwise null. |

PeerMessage shape:
| Field    | Type   | Description |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| clientId | number | Numeric ID of the peer that sent this message. |
| data     | string | The raw string payload received from that peer. |

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Echo server — broadcast each message back to all peers
--------
```tsx
import { useEffect } from 'react';
import { usePeerServer, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

function EchoServer() {
  const { ready, peers, broadcast, lastMessage, error } = usePeerServer(9050);

  useEffect(() => {
    if (lastMessage) {
      broadcast(`[${lastMessage.clientId}]: ${lastMessage.data}`);
    }
  }, [lastMessage]);

  return (
    <Box flexDirection="column" gap={4} padding={12}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Server: {ready ? 'Ready' : 'Starting...'}</Text>
      <Text fontSize={13}>Peers connected: {peers.length}</Text>
      {error && <Text fontSize={12}>Error: {error}</Text>}
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d

EXAMPLE: Reply only to the peer that sent a message
--------
```tsx
import { useEffect } from 'react';
import { usePeerServer, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

interface Command {
  action: string;
  value: number;
}

function PingServer() {
  const { ready, peers, send, lastMessage } = usePeerServer(8080);

  useEffect(() => {
    if (!lastMessage) return;
    const cmd: Command = JSON.parse(lastMessage.data);
    if (cmd.action === 'ping') {
      send(lastMessage.clientId, JSON.stringify({ action: 'pong', value: cmd.value }));
    }
  }, [lastMessage]);

  return (
    <Box flexDirection="column" gap={4} padding={12}>
      <Text fontSize={15}>Ping Server</Text>
      <Text fontSize={13}>Status: {ready ? 'Listening on :8080' : 'Binding...'}</Text>
      <Text fontSize={13}>Players: {peers.length}</Text>
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d

EXAMPLE: Broadcast world state to all peers whenever it changes
--------
```tsx
import { useEffect } from 'react';
import { usePeerServer, useLoveState, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

interface WorldState {
  tick: number;
  entities: { id: number; x: number; y: number }[];
}

function WorldServer() {
  const { ready, peers, broadcast } = usePeerServer(9050);
  const [world] = useLoveState<WorldState>('world', { tick: 0, entities: [] });

  useEffect(() => {
    if (ready && peers.length > 0) {
      broadcast(JSON.stringify(world));
    }
  }, [world, ready]);

  return (
    <Box flexDirection="column" gap={4} padding={12}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Tick: {world.tick}</Text>
      <Text fontSize={13}>Peers: {peers.length}</Text>
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d

EXAMPLE: Conditional server — activate only when hosting is toggled on
--------
```tsx
import { useState } from 'react';
import { usePeerServer, Box, Text, Pressable } from '@reactjit/core';

function HostToggle() {
  const [hosting, setHosting] = useState(false);
  const { ready, peers, error } = usePeerServer(hosting ? 9050 : null);

  return (
    <Box flexDirection="column" gap={8} padding={12}>
      <Pressable onPress={() => setHosting(h => !h)}>
        <Box backgroundColor={hosting ? '#a22' : '#2a7'} padding={8} borderRadius={4}>
          <Text fontSize={14}>{hosting ? 'Stop Hosting' : 'Start Hosting'}</Text>
        </Box>
      </Pressable>
      {hosting && (
        <Box flexDirection="column" gap={4}>
          <Text fontSize={13}>Server: {ready ? 'Ready on :9050' : 'Starting...'}</Text>
          <Text fontSize={13}>Connected peers: {peers.length}</Text>
          {error && <Text fontSize={12}>Error: {error}</Text>}
        </Box>
      )}
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: love2d

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- useWebSocket
- useLove
- useLoveSend


========================================
usePixelArt
========================================

Category: Hooks | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Convert Unicode symbol names or custom string art into memoized Box-based pixel geometry.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

usePixelArt converts a built-in symbol name or an array of string art lines into a memoized React element made entirely from <Box> components. Because Love2D's default font cannot render Unicode symbols (arrows, checkmarks, hearts, etc.), this hook is the correct way to display icons and pixel-art graphics across all targets. Any non-space character in the art grid is treated as a filled pixel; spaces are transparent. The result is memoized via useMemo and only recalculates when the art source or options change.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

```tsx
import { usePixelArt, PixelArt, getPixelArtSymbols } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Hook signature:
```tsx
usePixelArt(art: string | string[], options?: PixelArtOptions): React.ReactElement
```

Parameters:

| Parameter     | Type     | Default   | Description                                                          |
|---------------|----------|-----------|----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| art           | string   | —         | Built-in symbol name (e.g. 'play', 'heart', 'arrow-up')             |
| art           | string[] | —         | Custom string art lines. Any non-space char = filled pixel.          |
| options.size  | number   | 4         | Width and height of each pixel in logical units.                     |
| options.color | string   | '#ffffff' | Fill color for all pixels. Overridden per-row by options.colors.     |
| options.colors| string[] | undefined | Per-row fill colors. Row index maps to art row. Falls back to color. |
| options.gap   | number   | 0         | Gap in logical units between pixels (horizontal and vertical).       |

Returns: React.ReactElement — a column Box containing row Boxes of sized pixel Boxes.

Built-in symbol names:
play, pause, stop, skip-next, skip-prev, check, close, arrow-up, arrow-down,
arrow-left, arrow-right, circle, circle-outline, dot, plus, minus, heart, star,
volume, mute, chevron-right, chevron-left, menu

Component variant (declarative):
```tsx
<PixelArt art="play" size={4} color="#fff" style={containerStyle} />
```

PixelArtProps extends PixelArtOptions and adds:

| Prop  | Type             | Description                                    |
|-------|------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| art   | string | string[] | Symbol name or array of string art lines. |
| style | Style            | Optional container Box style overrides.        |

Utility function:
```tsx
getPixelArtSymbols(): string[]
// Returns the list of all built-in symbol names.
```

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Built-in play icon in a media control bar
--------
```tsx
import { usePixelArt } from '@reactjit/core';

function PlayButton({ onPress }: { onPress: () => void }) {
  const icon = usePixelArt('play', { size: 4, color: '#ffffff' });

  return (
    <Box
      onPress={onPress}
      style={{
        backgroundColor: '#1db954',
        padding: 10,
        borderRadius: 24,
        alignItems: 'center',
        justifyContent: 'center',
      }}
    >
      {icon}
    </Box>
  );
}
```
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: State-toggled mute/volume icon
--------
```tsx
import { useState } from 'react';
import { usePixelArt } from '@reactjit/core';

function VolumeControl({ onToggle }: { onToggle: (muted: boolean) => void }) {
  const [muted, setMuted] = useState(false);
  const icon = usePixelArt(muted ? 'mute' : 'volume', {
    size: 4,
    color: muted ? '#888888' : '#ffffff',
  });

  const handlePress = () => {
    setMuted(prev => {
      onToggle(!prev);
      return !prev;
    });
  };

  return (
    <Box onPress={handlePress} style={{ padding: 8 }}>
      {icon}
    </Box>
  );
}
```

SEE ALSO
---------

- Box — the underlying primitive used for all pixel rendering
