    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <Box
        style={{ width, height: 80, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa', borderRadius: 8 }}
        onClick={() => setExpanded(!expanded)}
      >
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#1e1e2e' }}>
          {expanded ? 'Collapse' : 'Expand'}
        </Text>
      </Box>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Bouncy spring
--------
import { useState } from 'react';
import { useSpring, Box } from '@reactjit/core';

function BouncyButton() {
  const [pressed, setPressed] = useState(false);
  const scale = useSpring(pressed ? 0.85 : 1.0, { stiffness: 400, damping: 8, mass: 0.5 });

  return (
    <Box
      style={{
        width: 120,
        height: 120,
        backgroundColor: '#f38ba8',
        borderRadius: 60,
        transform: { scaleX: scale, scaleY: scale },
      }}
      onClick={() => setPressed(!pressed)}
    />
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Smooth height transition
--------
import { useState } from 'react';
import { useSpring, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

function AccordionItem({ title, content }: { title: string; content: string }) {
  const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);
  const height = useSpring(open ? 120 : 0, { stiffness: 200, damping: 25 });

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%' }}>
      <Box
        style={{ width: '100%', height: 40, backgroundColor: '#313244', padding: 8 }}
        onClick={() => setOpen(!open)}
      >
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>{title}</Text>
      </Box>
      <Box style={{ width: '100%', height, overflow: 'hidden', backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e' }}>
        <Box style={{ padding: 8 }}>
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#a6adc8' }}>{content}</Text>
        </Box>
      </Box>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* Animation is skipped on first render -- value starts at targetValue immediately
* A new spring animation starts every time targetValue changes
* The previous animation is automatically stopped when a new one starts
* Config changes do NOT restart the animation -- only targetValue changes trigger animation
* Config is read from a ref at animation start time, so it can be dynamic
* The returned value triggers React re-render every animation frame

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- usetransition.txt -- Timing-based convenience hook (alternative to spring)
- useanimation.txt -- Full control over AnimatedValue
- animatedvalue.txt -- AnimatedValue spring configuration details


========================================
useTransition
========================================

Category: Animation | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Convenience hook that automatically transitions to a value with timing-based easing.

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

useTransition is a convenience hook that automatically starts a timing-based animation whenever its target value changes. It is the simplest way to add eased transitions to a component -- you provide the target value and optional timing configuration, and the hook returns the current interpolated value.

Like useSpring, the hook skips animation on the first render (the value starts at the target immediately). On subsequent renders where the target changes, a timing animation with the specified duration and easing is started.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Signature:
  function useTransition(value: number, config?: { duration?: number; easing?: EasingFunction }): number

Parameters:
  value: number
    The target value to transition towards. When this changes, a new timing animation starts.

  config?: object (optional)
    duration?: number            Duration in milliseconds. Default: 300
    easing?: EasingFunction      Easing function. Default: Easing.easeInOut

Returns:
  number
    The current interpolated value. Updates each animation frame, triggering re-render.

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Smooth opacity transition
--------
import { useState } from 'react';
import { useTransition, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

function FadeToggle() {
  const [visible, setVisible] = useState(true);
  const opacity = useTransition(visible ? 1 : 0, { duration: 400 });

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
      <Box style={{ width: 200, height: 80, opacity, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa', borderRadius: 8 }}>
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#1e1e2e' }}>Content</Text>
      </Box>
      <Box
        style={{ width: 100, height: 40, marginTop: 12, backgroundColor: '#313244', borderRadius: 4 }}
        onClick={() => setVisible(!visible)}
      >
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Toggle</Text>
      </Box>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Custom easing
--------
import { useState } from 'react';
import { useTransition, Easing, Box } from '@reactjit/core';

function SlidePanel() {
  const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);
  const x = useTransition(open ? 0 : -250, {
    duration: 500,
    easing: Easing.bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1),
  });

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
      <Box style={{
        width: 250,
        height: '100%',
        backgroundColor: '#313244',
        transform: { translateX: x },
      }}>
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#cdd6f4', padding: 16 }}>Side Panel</Text>
      </Box>
      <Box
        style={{ position: 'absolute', top: 10, right: 10, width: 80, height: 40, backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1', borderRadius: 4 }}
        onClick={() => setOpen(!open)}
      >
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#1e1e2e' }}>Menu</Text>
      </Box>
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

EXAMPLE: Progress bar animation
--------
import { useTransition, Box, Text } from '@reactjit/core';
import { Easing } from '@reactjit/core';

function ProgressBar({ progress }: { progress: number }) {
  const width = useTransition(progress * 300, { duration: 600, easing: Easing.easeOut });

  return (
    <Box style={{ width: 300, height: 24, backgroundColor: '#313244', borderRadius: 4 }}>
      <Box style={{
        width,
        height: '100%',
        backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1',
        borderRadius: 4,
      }} />
    </Box>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: all

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* Animation is skipped on first render -- value starts at the target immediately
* A new timing animation starts every time value changes
* The previous animation is automatically stopped when a new one starts
* Config changes do NOT restart the animation -- only value changes trigger animation
* Config is read from a ref at animation start time, so it can be dynamic
* Default easing is Easing.easeInOut; default duration is 300ms

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- usespring.txt -- Spring-based convenience hook (physics-based alternative)
- useanimation.txt -- Full control over AnimatedValue
- easing.txt -- Available easing functions


========================================
Routing
========================================

Category: Routing | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Brief overview of the @reactjit/router package for in-app navigation

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

ReactJIT includes a @reactjit/router package for in-app navigation between screens. Since there is no browser URL bar or history API in most targets, routing is purely in-memory. The router manages a stack of screens and provides hooks for navigating between them.

The router follows a simple pattern: define your screens as React components, register them with the router, and use navigation hooks to push, pop, or replace screens.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

Basic Usage Pattern:
  import { Router, Screen, useNavigation } from '@reactjit/router';

  function App() {
    return (
      <Router>
        <Screen name="home" component={HomeScreen} />
        <Screen name="settings" component={SettingsScreen} />
      </Router>
    );
  }

  function HomeScreen() {
    const nav = useNavigation();
    return (
      <Pressable onPress={() => nav.push('settings')}>
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 16 }}>Go to Settings</Text>
      </Pressable>
    );
  }

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- 05-components/pressable.txt - Navigation triggers
- 01-getting-started/philosophy.txt - Design principles


========================================
Router
========================================

Category: Routing | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Client-side routing for ReactJIT apps across all targets

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

@reactjit/router provides client-side routing with no external dependencies. It works on every ReactJIT target by abstracting history management behind a HistoryAdapter interface: memory-based for native targets, browser-based for web. Route matching supports named parameters (:id), optional parameters (:id?), and wildcards (*), with highest-specificity-wins semantics when multiple routes match.

Import: import { RouterProvider, Routes, Route, Link, Navigate, Outlet, useRouter, useNavigate, useLocation, useParams, useRoute, createMemoryHistory, createBrowserHistory } from '@reactjit/router';

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

---- createMemoryHistory(options?) ----------------------------------------

Creates an in-memory history stack. Use this on all non-web targets (love2d,
terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome).

  createMemoryHistory(options?: MemoryHistoryOptions): HistoryAdapter

  MemoryHistoryOptions:
    initialEntries?: string[]   // Initial path stack. Default: ['/']
    initialIndex?:  number      // Active index into initialEntries.
                                // Default: last entry.

  Returns: HistoryAdapter — pass to <RouterProvider history={...} />

---- createBrowserHistory() -----------------------------------------------

Creates a history adapter backed by the browser's History API. Web target only.
Listens to popstate for back/forward button support.

  createBrowserHistory(): HistoryAdapter

---- RouterProvider --------------------------------------------------------

Context provider. Wrap your entire app (or the routed subtree) with this once.
All routing hooks and components require a RouterProvider ancestor.

  <RouterProvider history={HistoryAdapter}>
    {children}
  </RouterProvider>

  Props:
    history   HistoryAdapter   Required. From createMemoryHistory() or
                               createBrowserHistory().
    children  ReactNode        Required.

---- Routes ----------------------------------------------------------------

Container that evaluates all child <Route> elements and renders the single
best-matching one. "Best" means highest specificity score among all patterns
that match the current pathname. Use this for exclusive routing.

  <Routes>
    {children}   // One or more <Route> elements
  </Routes>

---- Route -----------------------------------------------------------------

Declares a route. Must be a direct child of <Routes>. Has no visible output
on its own — Routes does the rendering.

  <Route path={string} element={ReactNode} />

  Props:
    path     string      Route pattern. Supports:
                           /users           — static segment
                           /users/:id       — named parameter
                           /users/:id?      — optional parameter
                           /files/*         — wildcard (rest of path)
                           *                — catch-all / 404 fallback
    element  ReactNode   What to render when this route matches.

  Specificity scoring (higher = matched first):
    static segment   4 points
    required param   3 points
    optional param   2 points
    wildcard         1 point

---- Link ------------------------------------------------------------------

Navigable wrapper. Calls navigate() on click. On web it renders as <a> so
the browser gets hover state, right-click, and native link behaviors.

  <Link to={string} replace? style? {...rest}>
    {children}
  </Link>

  Props:
    to        string     Required. Target path.
    replace?  boolean    Replace current history entry instead of pushing.
                         Default: false.
    style?    object     Style object forwarded to the underlying element.
    children  ReactNode  Required.

---- Navigate (declarative redirect) ----------------------------------------

When rendered, immediately navigates to `to`. Useful inside route elements for
conditional redirects or deprecated-path forwarding.

  <Navigate to={string} replace? />

  Props:
    to       string    Required. Target path.
    replace? boolean   Use replace instead of push. Default: false.

---- Outlet ----------------------------------------------------------------

Renders nested route content. Place inside a parent route's element component
to mark where child routes should appear. Backed by OutletContext.

  <Outlet />

  No props.

---- useRouter() -----------------------------------------------------------

Returns the full router state object. Prefer the focused hooks (useNavigate,
useLocation, useParams) unless you need everything at once.

  useRouter(): RouterState

  RouterState:
    location  Location                                    Current location.
    params    Record<string, string>                      Matched route params.
    navigate  (to: string, opts?: NavigateOptions) => void
    back      () => void
    forward   () => void

  Throws if called outside a <RouterProvider>.

---- useNavigate() ---------------------------------------------------------

Returns the navigate function. The most common hook for imperative navigation.

  useNavigate(): (to: string, options?: NavigateOptions) => void

  NavigateOptions:
    replace?  boolean   Replace history entry instead of pushing.

  Throws if called outside a <RouterProvider>.

---- useLocation() ---------------------------------------------------------

Returns the current Location object. Re-renders the component whenever the
location changes.

  useLocation(): Location

  Location:
    pathname  string   The path portion (e.g. '/users/42').
    search    string   Query string (e.g. '?tab=profile').
    hash      string   Hash fragment (e.g. '#bio').

  Throws if called outside a <RouterProvider>.

---- useParams<T>() --------------------------------------------------------

Returns the named URL parameters extracted from the matched route pattern.
Only populated inside a route rendered by <Routes> — parameters are provided
by RouteParamsContext.

  useParams<T extends Record<string, string> = Record<string, string>>(): T

  Returns: Object mapping parameter names to their decoded string values.
           Empty object if no parameters matched or used outside <Routes>.

  Example: route '/users/:id', pathname '/users/42' -> { id: '42' }

---- useRoute(pattern) -----------------------------------------------------

Imperatively match the current pathname against a pattern. Returns a tuple of
[matched, params]. Useful for conditional rendering without <Routes>.

  useRoute(pattern: string): [boolean, Record<string, string>]

  Arguments:
    pattern  string   Route pattern (same syntax as <Route path={...} />).

  Returns:
    [0]  boolean                 True if current pathname matches.
    [1]  Record<string, string>  Extracted params if matched, {} otherwise.

---- HistoryAdapter interface -----------------------------------------------

Implement this to add custom history backends (e.g., hash-based routing).

  interface HistoryAdapter {
    readonly location: Location
    push(path: string): void
    replace(path: string): void
    back(): void
    forward(): void
    subscribe(listener: (location: Location) => void): () => void
  }

---- Low-level matching utilities -------------------------------------------

  matchRoute(pattern: string, pathname: string): RouteMatch
    RouteMatch: { matched: boolean; params: Record<string, string>; path: string }

  scorePattern(pattern: string): number
    Returns specificity score. Higher = more specific.

  findBestMatch(patterns: string[], pathname: string): RouteMatch | null
    Returns the highest-scoring match among all patterns, or null.

  parsePath(path: string): Location
    Parses '/users/42?tab=info#bio' into { pathname, search, hash }.

  locationToString(loc: Location): string
    Reconstructs a full path string from a Location object.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- useNavigate
- useLocation
- useParams
- useRoute


========================================
Routing
========================================

Category: Routing | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

URL-based navigation and route matching for all targets

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

The ReactJIT router provides declarative, URL-based navigation across all rendering targets. It uses a history adapter pattern to support both browser-based navigation (web) and in-memory navigation (Love2D, terminal, grid targets). Routes are matched via path patterns with parameter extraction, and the system provides both component-based navigation (Link) and imperative navigation (useNavigate).

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

RouterProvider
--------------
Top-level provider that supplies routing state to the component tree.

```tsx
import { RouterProvider, createMemoryHistory } from '@reactjit/router';

<RouterProvider history={createMemoryHistory()}>
  <App />
</RouterProvider>
```

Type signature:
```tsx
interface RouterProviderProps {
  history: HistoryAdapter;
  children: ReactNode;
}
```

Routes
------
Container that evaluates all child Route elements and renders the best match based on pattern specificity.

```tsx
import { Routes, Route } from '@reactjit/router';

<Routes>
  <Route path="/" element={<Home />} />
  <Route path="/users/:id" element={<UserPage />} />
  <Route path="/users/new" element={<NewUser />} />
  <Route path="*" element={<NotFound />} />
</Routes>
```

Route matching precedence (highest to lowest):
- Static segments (/users/new) — score: 4 per segment
- Required parameters (/users/:id) — score: 3 per segment
- Optional parameters (/users/:id?) — score: 2 per segment
- Wildcards (/*) — score: 1

Route
-----
Marker component that defines a path pattern and corresponding element.

```tsx
<Route path="/users/:id" element={<UserPage />} />
```

Type signature:
```tsx
interface RouteProps {
  path: string;
  element: ReactNode;
}
```

Path pattern syntax:
- /users/new — static path
- /users/:id — named parameter (captured in useParams)
- /users/:id? — optional parameter
- /files/* — wildcard (captures rest as $rest param)
- / — root path

Link
----
Navigable element. Renders as an anchor tag and navigates on click.

```tsx
import { Link } from '@reactjit/router';

<Link to="/about">About</Link>
<Link to="/users/123" replace>View User</Link>
```

Type signature:
```tsx
interface LinkProps {
  to: string;
  replace?: boolean;
  children: ReactNode;
  style?: any;
}
```

Navigate
--------
Declarative redirect component. Triggers navigation when rendered.

```tsx
import { Navigate } from '@reactjit/router';

<Route path="/old-path" element={<Navigate to="/new-path" replace />} />
```

Useful for redirects and conditional navigation:
```tsx
function ProtectedRoute() {
  const isAuthenticated = useAuth();
  if (!isAuthenticated) return <Navigate to="/login" />;
  return <Dashboard />;
}
```

Outlet
------
Renders nested route content. Used in parent route elements to define where child routes appear.

```tsx
import { Outlet } from '@reactjit/router';

function Layout() {
  return (
    <Box flexDirection="column" width="100%" height="100%">
      <NavBar />
      <Outlet />
    </Box>
  );
}

<Routes>
  <Route path="/" element={<Layout />}>
    <Route path="home" element={<Home />} />
    <Route path="about" element={<About />} />
  </Route>
</Routes>
```

useRouter
---------
Returns full router state including location, params, and navigation methods.

```tsx
import { useRouter } from '@reactjit/router';

function Component() {
  const { location, navigate, back, forward } = useRouter();

  return (
    <Text fontSize={14}>
      Current path: {location.pathname}
    </Text>
  );
}
```

Type signature:
```tsx
interface RouterState {
  location: Location;
  params: Record<string, string>;
  navigate: (to: string, options?: NavigateOptions) => void;
  back: () => void;
  forward: () => void;
}

interface Location {
  pathname: string;
  search: string;
  hash: string;
}

interface NavigateOptions {
  replace?: boolean;
}
```

useNavigate
-----------
Returns navigation function for imperative navigation.

```tsx
import { useNavigate } from '@reactjit/router';

function Component() {
  const navigate = useNavigate();

  const handleSubmit = () => {
    // Save data...
    navigate('/success');
  };

  const handleCancel = () => {
    navigate('/home', { replace: true });
  };

  return <Form onSubmit={handleSubmit} />;
}
```

Type signature:
```tsx
function useNavigate(): (to: string, options?: NavigateOptions) => void
```

useLocation
-----------
Returns current location object.

```tsx
import { useLocation } from '@reactjit/router';

function Component() {
  const location = useLocation();

  return (
    <Box>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Path: {location.pathname}</Text>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Query: {location.search}</Text>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Hash: {location.hash}</Text>
    </Box>
  );
}
```

Type signature:
```tsx
function useLocation(): Location
```

useParams
---------
Returns route parameters extracted from the current path.

```tsx
import { useParams } from '@reactjit/router';

function UserPage() {
  const params = useParams<{ id: string }>();

  return <Text fontSize={16}>User ID: {params.id}</Text>;
}

// Route definition:
<Route path="/users/:id" element={<UserPage />} />

// When navigating to /users/123, params.id === "123"
```

Type signature:
```tsx
function useParams<T extends Record<string, string> = Record<string, string>>(): T
```

useRoute
--------
Matches current location against a pattern. Returns [matched, params] tuple.

```tsx
import { useRoute } from '@reactjit/router';

function Component() {
  const [isUserPage, params] = useRoute('/users/:id');

  if (isUserPage) {
    return <Text fontSize={14}>Viewing user {params.id}</Text>;
  }
  return <Text fontSize={14}>Not a user page</Text>;
}
```

Type signature:
```tsx
function useRoute(pattern: string): [boolean, Record<string, string>]
```

createMemoryHistory
-------------------
Creates an in-memory history adapter for non-browser environments.

```tsx
import { createMemoryHistory } from '@reactjit/router';

const history = createMemoryHistory({
  initialEntries: ['/home', '/about'],
  initialIndex: 1, // Start at /about
});
```

Type signature:
```tsx
interface MemoryHistoryOptions {
  initialEntries?: string[];
  initialIndex?: number;
}

function createMemoryHistory(options?: MemoryHistoryOptions): HistoryAdapter
```

createBrowserHistory
--------------------
Creates a browser history adapter using pushState/replaceState (web only).

```tsx
import { createBrowserHistory } from '@reactjit/router';

const history = createBrowserHistory();
```

Type signature:
```tsx
function createBrowserHistory(): HistoryAdapter
```

HistoryAdapter Interface
-------------------------
All history implementations conform to this interface.

```tsx
interface HistoryAdapter {
  readonly location: Location;
  push(path: string): void;
  replace(path: string): void;
  back(): void;
  forward(): void;
  subscribe(listener: (location: Location) => void): () => void;
}
```

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* Always wrap your app in RouterProvider with a history adapter
* Use createMemoryHistory for non-browser targets (Love2D, terminal, grid)
* Use createBrowserHistory for web targets
* Routes must be children of Routes component for best-match semantics
* Static routes (/users/new) are always matched before parameterized routes (/users/:id)
* Path patterns are normalized — trailing slashes are removed (except for "/")
* useParams only works inside a Route element's component tree
* useRouter throws an error if used outside RouterProvider
* Link components must have a "to" prop — empty strings navigate to "/"
* Navigate triggers on mount — wrap in conditional rendering for guarded navigation
* Optional parameters (:id?) match both /users and /users/123
* Wildcards (*) capture the rest of the path as $rest param
* Route matching is case-sensitive
* Query strings and hashes are preserved but not used for route matching
* Multiple Routes components can coexist (separate routing contexts)
* Outlet only renders content when used with nested route definitions
* back() and forward() do nothing at history boundaries (beginning/end of stack)
* replace: true navigation replaces current entry without growing history stack
* RouterProvider's history prop must be stable — don't recreate on every render

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- State Management — useContext for sharing route-derived state
- Event Handling — Pressable and onClick for custom navigation UI
- Layout Components — Box, FlexRow for navigation bars and route containers
- Hooks — useState for client-side route state, useEffect for navigation side effects
- Animation — useSpring for route transition animations
- Modal — Navigate inside modals for dialog routing patterns
- TextInput — Form navigation with query parameters
- ScrollView — Per-route scroll state management


========================================
Targets
========================================

Category: Targets | Difficulty: beginner | Platforms: love2d, web, terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome

Hub page listing all seven ReactJIT render targets

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

ReactJIT renders to seven distinct targets. Each target has its own painter, transport mechanism, and build configuration. The core component API (Box, Text, Image, Pressable, and all higher-level components) works across all of them. The differences are in rendering fidelity, color support, input capabilities, and distribution format.

Choose your target based on your deployment environment. Love2D is the primary target with the most features. Web mode runs in browsers. The grid targets (terminal, CC, Neovim, HS, Awesome) operate on character grids or pixel canvases within their host applications.

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Target Comparison
--------
Target          | Transport    | Colors     | Input              | Build Format
----------------|-------------|------------|--------------------|--------------
Love2D          | QuickJS FFI | Truecolor  | Mouse/KB/Touch/Pad | IIFE bundle
Web             | Module.FS   | Full CSS   | Mouse/KB/Touch     | ESM bundle
Terminal        | (built-in)  | Truecolor  | Keyboard           | Node.js ESM
ComputerCraft   | WebSocket   | 16 colors  | Keyboard           | Node.js ESM
Neovim          | stdio       | 256 color  | Keyboard           | Node.js ESM
Hammerspoon     | WebSocket   | Truecolor  | Mouse/Keyboard     | Node.js ESM
AwesomeWM       | stdio       | Truecolor  | Mouse/Keyboard     | Node.js ESM
--------
Platforms: all

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- love2d.txt - Love2D target (primary, most mature)
- web.txt - Web/browser target
- terminal.txt - Terminal emulator target
- computercraft.txt - ComputerCraft (Minecraft) target
- neovim.txt - Neovim floating windows target
- hammerspoon.txt - Hammerspoon (macOS) target
- awesomewm.txt - AwesomeWM (Linux) target


========================================
AwesomeWM Target
========================================

Category: Targets | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: awesome

ReactJIT renderer for AwesomeWM Linux tiling window manager widgets

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

The AwesomeWM target (@reactjit/awesome) renders ReactJIT components as widgets in the Awesome tiling window manager on Linux. Rendering uses Cairo for 2D graphics and Pango for text shaping, giving high-quality output with full antialiasing. Communication happens via stdio between the Node.js process and the AwesomeWM Lua runtime.

This target lets you build status bars, notification panels, system dashboards, or any custom widget that integrates into your AwesomeWM desktop.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

esbuild Configuration:
  --platform=node
  --format=esm

Requirements:
  AwesomeWM running on Linux.
  The Node.js process is spawned by AwesomeWM and communicates via stdio.

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: AwesomeWM Widget Entry Point
--------
import { createAwesomeApp } from '@reactjit/awesome';
import App from './App';

const app = createAwesomeApp();
app.render(<App />);
--------
Platforms: awesome

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- 09-targets/hammerspoon.txt - Similar desktop-integration target for macOS
- 09-targets/love2d.txt - Primary pixel-level target
- 02-architecture/transport.txt - stdio transport details


========================================
ComputerCraft Target
========================================

Category: Targets | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: cc

ReactJIT renderer for Minecraft ComputerCraft Tweaked terminals

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

The ComputerCraft target (@reactjit/cc) renders ReactJIT components inside Minecraft via the ComputerCraft: Tweaked mod. The CC terminal provides a 51x19 character display with a fixed 16-color palette. Communication between the Node.js process (running your React app) and the in-game terminal happens over WebSocket.

This is the most constrained target in terms of display capabilities, but it demonstrates the framework's multi-target architecture: the same React component model works even on a 16-color 51x19 character grid inside a video game.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

esbuild Configuration:
  --platform=node
  --format=esm
  --external:ws

Display Constraints:
  Width:  51 characters
  Height: 19 characters
  Colors: 16 (CC palette: white, orange, magenta, lightBlue, yellow, lime,
          pink, gray, lightGray, cyan, purple, blue, brown, green, red, black)

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: CC App Setup
--------
import { createCCApp } from '@reactjit/cc';
import App from './App';

const app = createCCApp({
  port: 8080,  // WebSocket port for CC to connect to
});

app.render(<App />);
--------
Platforms: cc

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* Always include --external:ws in the esbuild configuration. The ws package
* uses native Node.js modules that cannot be bundled.
* Design for the 51x19 grid. Components that work on larger displays will
* overflow or truncate on CC terminals.
* Use the 16-color palette consciously. Subtle color differences will be
* lost when mapped to the nearest palette entry.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- 09-targets/terminal.txt - Similar grid target without color constraint
- 09-targets/neovim.txt - Another grid target
- 02-architecture/transport.txt - WebSocket transport details


========================================
Hammerspoon Target
========================================

Category: Targets | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: hs

ReactJIT renderer for Hammerspoon macOS automation overlays

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

The Hammerspoon target (@reactjit/hs) renders ReactJIT components as pixel-perfect overlays on macOS using Hammerspoon's hs.canvas API. Communication happens over WebSocket between the Node.js process (running your React app) and the Hammerspoon Lua runtime. This lets you build rich floating dashboards, HUDs, or status panels that render directly on your macOS desktop.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

esbuild Configuration:
  --platform=node
  --format=esm
  --external:ws

Requirements:
  Hammerspoon installed on macOS.
  WebSocket connection from Hammerspoon Lua to the Node.js server.

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Hammerspoon App Entry Point
--------
import { createHSApp } from '@reactjit/hs';
import App from './App';

const app = createHSApp({
  port: 9090,  // WebSocket port
});

app.render(<App />);
--------
Platforms: hs

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* Always include --external:ws in the esbuild configuration.
* The Hammerspoon Lua side must initiate the WebSocket connection.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- 09-targets/love2d.txt - Primary pixel-level target

========================================
Love2D Target
========================================

Category: Targets | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: love2d

The primary ReactJIT target - pixel-perfect rendering via the Love2D game engine

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

Love2D is the primary and most mature ReactJIT target. Your React components run inside QuickJS (a lightweight JavaScript engine embedded in the Love2D process via libquickjs.so). The Lua runtime handles layout computation and rendering using love.graphics draw calls. This gives you pixel-perfect rendering with full support for mouse, keyboard, touch, and gamepad input.

The Love2D target supports the complete ReactJIT feature set: flexbox layout, text measurement and wrapping, image loading, scroll containers, gradients, shadows, transforms, transitions, keyframe animations, and the F12 visual inspector.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

esbuild Configuration:
  --format=iife
  --global-name=ReactLove

Build Commands:
  reactjit dev              - Watch mode with HMR
  reactjit build            - Lint gate + production bundle
  reactjit build dist:love  - Self-extracting Linux binary

Runtime Modes:
  native  - QuickJS FFI bridge (default for `love .`)
  web     - Module.FS bridge (for love.js / Emscripten builds)
  canvas  - Module.FS bridge + native rendering pipeline (hybrid web)

Love2D Callbacks (wired in main.lua):
  love.update(dt)       -> ReactLove.update(dt)
  love.draw()           -> ReactLove.draw()
  love.mousepressed()   -> ReactLove.mousepressed(x, y, button)
  love.mousereleased()  -> ReactLove.mousereleased(x, y, button)
  love.mousemoved()     -> ReactLove.mousemoved(x, y)
  love.keypressed()     -> ReactLove.keypressed(key, scancode, isrepeat)
  love.keyreleased()    -> ReactLove.keyreleased(key, scancode)
  love.textinput()      -> ReactLove.textinput(text)
  love.wheelmoved()     -> ReactLove.wheelmoved(x, y)
  love.touchpressed()   -> ReactLove.touchpressed(id, x, y, dx, dy, pressure)
  love.touchreleased()  -> ReactLove.touchreleased(id, x, y, dx, dy, pressure)
  love.touchmoved()     -> ReactLove.touchmoved(id, x, y, dx, dy, pressure)
  love.gamepadpressed() -> ReactLove.gamepadpressed(joystick, button)
  love.gamepadreleased()-> ReactLove.gamepadreleased(joystick, button)
  love.gamepadaxis()    -> ReactLove.gamepadaxis(joystick, axis, value)
  love.resize()         -> ReactLove.resize(w, h)
  love.quit()           -> ReactLove.quit()

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Standard App Entry Point (src/index.tsx)
--------
import { createLove2DApp } from '@reactjit/native';
import App from './App';

const app = createLove2DApp();
app.render(<App />);
--------
Platforms: love2d

EXAMPLE: main.lua Entry Point
--------
local ReactLove = require("lua")

function love.load()
  ReactLove.init({
    bundlePath = "bundle.js",
    libpath = "lib/libquickjs",
  })
end

function love.update(dt)
  ReactLove.update(dt)
end

function love.draw()
  ReactLove.draw()
end

function love.mousepressed(x, y, button)
  ReactLove.mousepressed(x, y, button)
end

function love.mousereleased(x, y, button)
  ReactLove.mousereleased(x, y, button)
end

function love.mousemoved(x, y)
  ReactLove.mousemoved(x, y)
end

function love.keypressed(key, scancode, isrepeat)
  ReactLove.keypressed(key, scancode, isrepeat)
end

function love.keyreleased(key, scancode)
  ReactLove.keyreleased(key, scancode)
end

function love.textinput(text)
  ReactLove.textinput(text)
end

function love.wheelmoved(x, y)
  ReactLove.wheelmoved(x, y)
end

function love.resize(w, h)
  ReactLove.resize(w, h)
end

function love.quit()
  ReactLove.quit()
end
--------
Platforms: love2d

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

Features:
- Pixel-perfect rendering (no browser rendering quirks)
- Full input support: mouse, keyboard, touch, gamepad
- F12 visual inspector for debugging layout
- Backtick (`) console for evaluating JS/Lua at runtime
- Hot module reload (HMR) -- bundle.js changes detected automatically
- Screenshot capture via `reactjit screenshot`
- Error overlay for runtime errors
- Self-extracting binary distribution (Linux)

Limitations:
- No text selection or clipboard (OS-level)
- Font rendering is Love2D's built-in renderer (no HarfBuzz)
- Bold is simulated (faux bold via double draw with 0.8px offset)
- No sub-pixel antialiasing

---------
CRITICAL RULES
---------

* The bundle format MUST be IIFE with --global-name=ReactLove.
* ESM or CJS bundles will not load in QuickJS.
* Always use the CLI for builds: `reactjit build`, not raw esbuild.
* The Lua runtime files must be present locally for `love .` to work.
* Run `reactjit update` after any framework changes.

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- 02-architecture/pipeline.txt - How the frame loop works
- 02-architecture/painter.txt - Love2D painter details
- 10-advanced/lua-runtime.txt - All 19 Lua modules
- 10-advanced/debugging.txt - F12 inspector and console


========================================
Neovim Target
========================================

Category: Targets | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: nvim

ReactJIT renderer that outputs to Neovim floating windows

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

The Neovim target (@reactjit/nvim) renders ReactJIT components as Neovim floating windows. Communication happens via stdio using the neovim npm package. The grid layout engine computes character-cell positions, and the painter creates and updates floating windows with the appropriate content and highlight groups.

This target lets you build rich UI panels, dashboards, or interactive tools that run directly inside your Neovim editor.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

esbuild Configuration:
  --platform=node
  --format=esm

Requirements:
  Neovim 0.7+ with the `neovim` npm package installed.
  Communication via stdio (the Node.js process is spawned by Neovim).

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Neovim Plugin Entry Point
--------
import { createNvimApp } from '@reactjit/nvim';
import App from './App';

const app = createNvimApp();
app.render(<App />);
--------
Platforms: nvim

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

Neovim floating windows support:
- Text content with highlight groups for coloring
- Borders (single, double, rounded, etc.)
- Positioning relative to the editor window
- Transparency via winblend option

Limitations:
- No pixel-level rendering (character grid only)
- Colors limited to Neovim's highlight group system
- Input is primarily keyboard-driven (Neovim key mappings)
- Window management is constrained by Neovim's floating window API

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- 09-targets/terminal.txt - Similar grid target for standalone terminals
- 09-targets/awesomewm.txt - Another stdio-based target
- 02-architecture/transport.txt - stdio transport details


========================================
Terminal Target
========================================

Category: Targets | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: terminal

Pure JavaScript terminal renderer using ANSI truecolor escape sequences

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

The terminal target (@reactjit/terminal) renders ReactJIT components to a terminal emulator using ANSI truecolor escape sequences. The layout is computed by the grid layout engine (@reactjit/grid), which maps flexbox concepts to a character-cell grid. Each cell contains a single character with foreground and background colors.

This target runs as a Node.js process and writes directly to stdout. It requires a terminal with truecolor support (24-bit color), which most modern terminals provide.

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

esbuild Configuration:
  --platform=node
  --format=esm

Build Commands:
  npm run build:terminal-demo       - Build terminal demo
  reactjit build dist:terminal    - Single-file Node.js executable

Grid Target Primitives:
  Terminal targets use lowercase JSX intrinsics:
    <view>  - Container element (equivalent to Box)
    <text>  - Text element (equivalent to Text)

  Define local wrappers for the standard Box/Text API:
    function Box(props) { return <view {...props} />; }
    function Text(props) { return <text {...props} />; }

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Terminal App Entry Point
--------
import { createTerminalApp } from '@reactjit/terminal';
import App from './App';

const app = createTerminalApp();
app.render(<App />);
--------
Platforms: terminal

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

Constraints:
- Character grid: no sub-pixel rendering, all positions snap to character cells
- Dimensions are measured in characters, not pixels
- Limited to what the terminal can display (no images, no gradients)
- Color depends on terminal support (truecolor preferred, fallback to 256)
- Input is keyboard-only in most terminals (no mouse by default)

Distribution:
- `reactjit build dist:terminal` produces a single-file Node.js executable
- The output can be run with `node dist/main.js`
- No system dependencies beyond Node.js

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- 09-targets/computercraft.txt - Similar grid target with 16-color constraint
- 09-targets/neovim.txt - Grid target inside Neovim
- 02-architecture/painter.txt - How terminal painting works


========================================
Web Target
========================================

Category: Targets | Difficulty: intermediate | Platforms: web

DOM overlay renderer that maps ReactJIT components to HTML/CSS elements

---------
OVERVIEW
---------

The web target (@reactjit/web) renders ReactJIT components as real DOM elements with CSS flexbox. The same Box, Text, and Image components that render as draw calls on Love2D render as div, span, and img elements in the browser. The RendererMode context switches the primitives between 'web' and 'native' behavior.

The web target can run standalone (a regular React web app) or alongside a Love2D canvas (as an overlay for web builds of Love2D games using Emscripten).

---------
API / SYNTAX
---------

esbuild Configuration:
  --format=esm

Element Mapping:
  <Box>       -> <div> with CSS flexbox
  <Text>      -> <span> with inline display
  <Image>     -> <img> with block display
  <Pressable> -> <div> with pointer events, tabIndex, role="button"

Style Conversion (styleToCSS function):
  Style.justifyContent 'start'/'end'  -> CSS 'flex-start'/'flex-end'
  Style.color [r,g,b,a] (0-1 range)  -> CSS rgba(R,G,B,A) (0-255 range)
  Style.backgroundGradient            -> CSS linear-gradient()
  Style.transform                     -> CSS transform string
  Style.transition                    -> CSS transition property
  Style.shadowColor/Offset/Blur      -> CSS box-shadow
  Style.textShadowColor/Offset       -> CSS text-shadow
  Style.outlineWidth/Color/Offset    -> CSS outline

---------
EXAMPLES
---------

EXAMPLE: Web-Only App
--------
import React from 'react';
import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client';
import { RendererProvider } from '@reactjit/core';
import App from './App';

const root = createRoot(document.getElementById('root')!);
root.render(
  <RendererProvider mode="web">
    <App />
  </RendererProvider>
);
--------
Platforms: web

EXAMPLE: Web Overlay with Love2D Canvas
--------
import { useLoveOverlays } from '@reactjit/core';

function GameOverlay({ canvasRef }) {
  const overlays = useLoveOverlays(canvasRef);

  return (
    <div style={{ position: 'absolute', top: 0, left: 0 }}>
      {overlays.map(o => (
        <div key={o.id} style={{
          position: 'absolute',
          left: o.domX,
          top: o.domY,
        }}>
          {/* DOM overlay positioned relative to Love2D coordinates */}
        </div>
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}
--------
Platforms: web

---------
PLATFORM NOTES
---------

Features:
- Full CSS flexbox (browser-native layout engine)
- CSS transitions and animations (mapped from Style.transition)
- Hover/active styles via React state (onPointerEnter/Leave, onPointerDown/Up)
- Drag support via pointer capture
- Keyboard accessibility (tabIndex on interactive elements)
- All DOM event types supported

Differences from Love2D:
- Layout is computed by the browser, not layout.lua
- Text measurement uses browser font metrics, not Love2D font:getWrap()
- The browser applies its own defaults (margins on body, etc.)
- Some visual properties may render slightly differently

---------
SEE ALSO
---------

- 09-targets/love2d.txt - Love2D target for comparison
- 02-architecture/painter.txt - How web primitives convert styles
- 12-api-reference/style-properties.txt - Style properties reference

Hub page for advanced ReactJIT documentation

---------
OVERVIEW

These topics assume familiarity with the rendering pipeline (see 02-architecture/) and the component API (see 05-components/).
